claims.claim: All paths in symbol IDs, atlas.json, and storage schema must use forward-slash separators; backslash paths are normalized at ingest via normalizePath().
claims.rationale: Ensures byte-identical symbol IDs across Windows and Linux developers on the same repo.
claims.excerpt: **Path normalization is required at ingest.** All paths in symbol IDs (and in atlas.json, and in the storage schema) use forward-slash separators, even on Windows.

claims.claim: Code review should flag any path-handling code that does not go through normalizePath().
claims.rationale: Prevents silent bifurcation of IDs across platforms.
claims.excerpt: Code review should flag any path-handling code that doesn't go through this utility.

claims.claim: Committed atlases from older tooling must remain readable by newer tooling within a major version.
claims.rationale: Format stability is the mechanism for cross-version compatibility.
claims.excerpt: **Committed atlases from older tooling must remain readable by newer tooling within a major version.** Format stability is the mechanism.

claims.claim: Consumers that need a line number must read the `line` field from the Symbol record and never parse it out of the ID.
claims.rationale: The Symbol record is the authoritative source of line information; parsing line from ID is broken code.
claims.excerpt: Consumers that need a line number (for jump-to-definition, log output, etc.) read the `line` field from the Symbol record, never parse it out of the ID. Any code that tries to extract line from ID is broken.

claims.claim: Language codes use short forms in IDs, defined authoritatively by the LANG_CODES constant in src/types.ts.
claims.rationale: Short codes save tokens across many occurrences; a single authoritative mapping avoids drift.
claims.excerpt: **Language codes use short forms in IDs.** The mapping is authoritative and lives as a single constant `LANG_CODES` in `src/types.ts`

claims.claim: Line numbers are supplementary metadata stored on the Symbol record, not part of the ID; moving a symbol within a file updates `line` but not the ID.
claims.rationale: Preserves ID stability across cosmetic/line-shifting edits while retaining line info for debugging and navigation.
claims.excerpt: **Line numbers are supplementary metadata, not identity.** Every `Symbol` record stores `line` as a field... But the ID itself is stable across line moves.

claims.claim: Moving a symbol to a different file or renaming it produces a new ID; incremental reindex rebinds claims as part of normal flow.
claims.rationale: File moves and renames are semantic changes that legitimately produce new IDs.
claims.excerpt: **Moving a symbol to a different file changes its ID.** ... incremental reindex rebinds claims as part of its normal flow.

claims.claim: New languages must add entries to LANG_CODES; adding entries is additive but changing existing short codes is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.
claims.rationale: LANG_CODES is stable public API embedded in symbol IDs.
claims.excerpt: **`LANG_CODES` is stable public API.** Adding entries is additive; changing existing short codes (e.g. renaming `ts` to `typescript`) is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.

claims.claim: Overloaded names in the same file collide on ID; the first declaration wins and subsequent overloads are not addressable via ID in MVP.
claims.rationale: Accepted MVP limitation; a disambiguator would be a future major-version change.
claims.excerpt: **Overloaded names in the same file collide.** Two symbols with the same name at different lines in the same file produce the same ID. For MVP, we accept this limitation

claims.claim: Reference IDs use the format ref:<lang>:<path>:<line> and include line because a reference is a site in a file.
claims.rationale: A reference is a site in a file, not an entity, so line is part of its identity.
claims.excerpt:
Reference IDs follow the same rule with `ref:` prefix and include line
(because a reference *is* a site in a file, not an entity)

claims.claim: Symbol IDs are persisted into atlas.json, a committed team artifact appearing in git history, PR diffs, and code review tooling.
claims.rationale: Explains why format stability matters — IDs are tokens committed to the repo, not just session handles.
claims.excerpt: Per ADR-06, symbol IDs are also persisted into `atlas.json`, the committed team artifact. This means IDs now appear in git history, PR diffs, and code review tooling.

claims.claim: Symbol IDs follow the fixed format sym:<lang>:<path>:<name>, keyed to path and name rather than line.
claims.rationale: Line numbers are volatile while symbol identity is semantic; using path+name prevents noisy atlas.json diffs on routine edits.
claims.excerpt:
Symbol IDs follow a fixed format keyed to **path + name**, not line:

```
sym:<lang>:<path>:<name>
```

claims.claim: The symbol ID format MUST NOT change in MVP or v0.x releases; a change requires a major version bump and explicit migration note.
claims.rationale: Format instability would break every downstream consumer silently and invalidate committed atlases.
claims.excerpt: The format MUST NOT change in MVP or v0.x releases. A change to the ID format requires a major version bump and an explicit migration note.

claims.claim: normalizePath() must be called at every ingest boundary (LSP paths, config parsing, atlas.json import, ADR directory scans).
claims.rationale: Forgetting it anywhere creates a bifurcation where some IDs use backslashes and others forward slashes, producing silent duplicate symbols.
claims.excerpt: **`normalizePath()` is a single enforcement point.** It must be called at every ingest boundary: reading file paths from LSP, parsing config, importing atlas.json, scanning ADR directories.

claims.claim: CI may mechanically enforce the invariant by grepping for @anthropic-ai/sdk imports outside src/extraction/.
claims.rationale: Indicates how the rule is enforced in practice.
claims.excerpt: A grep for imports of `@anthropic-ai/sdk` outside `src/extraction/` should return zero matches. CI may enforce this.

claims.claim: ContextAtlas separates expensive index-time reasoning from cheap query-time lookups.
claims.rationale: Explains the underlying architectural philosophy that this ADR protects.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas's value proposition rests on a clean separation: expensive reasoning happens at index time, cheap lookups happen at query time.

claims.claim: Features needing model reasoning should be moved to index time, replaced with deterministic heuristics, or exposed to the caller rather than invoked at query time.
claims.rationale: Provides the prescribed alternatives when a query-time model call would be natural but is disallowed.
claims.excerpt:
Move the work to index time (pre-compute and store)
  - Fall back to deterministic heuristics (pick first, truncate, etc.)
  - Expose the ambiguity to the caller and let Claude decide

claims.claim: Language adapters must not call the Anthropic API; they are local tooling wrappers only.
claims.rationale: Introducing model calls into the adapter layer would violate the query-time invariant.
claims.excerpt: Language adapters MUST NOT call the Anthropic API. They are local tooling wrappers (tsserver, Pyright); introducing model calls into the adapter layer would violate the query-time invariant.

claims.claim: Only the extraction pipeline (src/extraction/) may import @anthropic-ai/sdk or call the Anthropic API.
claims.rationale: Preserves the architectural invariant that expensive reasoning happens at index time, keeping query latency sub-100ms and query cost zero.
claims.excerpt: The extraction pipeline (`src/extraction/`) is the only module in the codebase permitted to import from `@anthropic-ai/sdk` or otherwise call the Anthropic API.

claims.claim: Query latency must stay sub-100ms and query cost must be zero.
claims.rationale: The architectural promise is 'pay once at index time'; model calls would add seconds and per-request costs.
claims.excerpt:
Query latency must stay sub-100ms. Model calls add seconds.
- Query cost must be zero. Model calls cost cents per request at scale.

claims.claim: Query-time code paths must not call the Anthropic API under any circumstances.
claims.rationale: Model calls at query time would break sub-100ms latency and zero-cost query guarantees.
claims.excerpt: Query-time code paths — MCP tool handlers, language adapters, storage layer, git integration, config parsing — MUST NOT call the Anthropic API under any circumstances.

claims.claim: Adapter loading must happen in a single bootstrap location (src/index.ts or src/adapters/registry.ts) which instantiates adapters based on config and injects them into core.
claims.rationale: Centralizing adapter instantiation enables dependency injection and keeps the plugin boundary clean.
claims.excerpt: Adapter loading happens in one place: the bootstrap code in `src/index.ts` (or a dedicated `src/adapters/registry.ts`). That single location reads the config's `languages` field, instantiates the corresponding adapters, and hands them to core via dependency injection.

claims.claim: Adapters must not share state with each other; each operates independently within its language.
claims.rationale: Independent operation preserves modularity and isolation between language plugins.
claims.excerpt: Adapters must not share state with each other. Each operates independently within its language.

claims.claim: Adding a new language adapter requires only a new file in src/adapters/ implementing LanguageAdapter, a single line in the registry, and tests; no other files should change.
claims.rationale: Ensures true pluggability and allows third-party contributors to add languages without modifying code they don't own.
claims.excerpt:
Adding a new language adapter requires:
1. A new file in `src/adapters/` implementing `LanguageAdapter`
2. One line in the adapter registry
3. Tests for the new adapter

No other file in the codebase should need to change.

claims.claim: ContextAtlas supports TypeScript and Python in MVP, with a roadmap for Java, Go, .NET, and Rust; each adapter wraps a specific language server.
claims.rationale: Motivates the plugin architecture: many languages will be added over time.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas supports TypeScript and Python in MVP, with a roadmap to Java, Go, .NET, and Rust. Each language adapter is a separate module that wraps a specific language server.

claims.claim: Core code may only import the LanguageAdapter interface from src/types.ts, not concrete adapter implementations.
claims.rationale: If core imports concrete adapters, the interface abstraction is ceremonial and language support stops being pluggable.
claims.excerpt: Core code (`src/mcp/`, `src/storage/`, `src/extraction/`) imports only the `LanguageAdapter` interface from `src/types.ts`. Core code MUST NOT import concrete adapter implementations.

claims.claim: Cross-adapter assumptions are disallowed; logic that works for one language but not another must either move into the adapter or be exposed via the interface.
claims.rationale: Language-specific behavior in core code indicates a design bug and breaks the abstraction.
claims.excerpt: Cross-adapter assumptions are disallowed. Code that only works for TypeScript but not Python has a design bug — either the logic belongs in the adapter, or the interface needs to expose the capability.

claims.claim: Functions needing a language adapter must receive it as a parameter via dependency injection, never import it directly.
claims.rationale: Dependency injection is required to preserve the plugin boundary.
claims.excerpt: Dependency injection pattern is required throughout. Functions that need a language adapter receive it as a parameter, never import it directly.

claims.claim: Tests may substitute mock adapters via dependency injection without monkey-patching.
claims.rationale: A benefit of the plugin architecture for testability.
claims.excerpt: Tests can substitute mock adapters without monkey-patching.

claims.claim: The LanguageAdapter interface is stable public API; changes must be treated like protocol changes.
claims.rationale: Changes to LanguageAdapter break every adapter implementation, including third-party ones.
claims.excerpt: The adapter interface is stable public API. Changes to `LanguageAdapter` break every adapter implementation. Treat interface changes like protocol changes.

claims.claim: Any new field added to bundles requires defining both its JSON shape and its compact representation
claims.rationale: Adding only one representation is a bug since both formats must stay in sync
claims.excerpt: Any new field added to bundles requires defining both its JSON shape and its compact representation. Adding only one is a bug.

claims.claim: Automated tools needing to parse bundle output should request format: "json" rather than parsing compact format
claims.rationale: The compact parser on the receiving side is informal and intended for LLM consumption, not formal parsing
claims.excerpt: If a future automated tool needs to parse compact output, it should request `format: "json"` instead.

claims.claim: Bundles should avoid huge responses; use progressive disclosure via IDs rather than switching format
claims.rationale: Bundle size advice applies to both formats; format choice doesn't solve size problems
claims.excerpt: Bundle size advice (avoid huge responses) applies to both formats. The fix is progressive disclosure via IDs, not format choice.

claims.claim: Changes to the compact grammar require a minor version bump
claims.rationale: The compact grammar is part of DESIGN.md and is a versioned contract
claims.excerpt: The compact grammar is part of DESIGN.md and changes require a minor version bump.

claims.claim: Compact text is the default output format for get_symbol_context and all future bundle-returning tools
claims.rationale: Token efficiency is the core value proposition; JSON is 40-60% more tokens than compact format
claims.excerpt: Compact text is the default output format for `get_symbol_context` and all future bundle-returning tools.

claims.claim: Every bundle returned by get_symbol_context is consumed by an LLM (Claude)
claims.rationale: Drives the decision to prioritize token efficiency in default output
claims.excerpt: Every bundle returned by `get_symbol_context` is consumed by an LLM (Claude). Token efficiency directly affects the tool's core value proposition

claims.claim: JSON is roughly 40-60% more tokens than compact text for equivalent bundle information
claims.rationale: Quantifies the token cost that motivated defaulting to compact format
claims.excerpt: JSON is roughly 40-60% more tokens than a well-designed compact text format encoding the same information.

claims.claim: JSON output is available by passing format: "json" in input parameters
claims.rationale: JSON must remain available for programmatic consumers like benchmark harnesses and future UI
claims.excerpt: JSON is available by passing `format: "json"` in the input parameters.

claims.claim: The JSON schema is stable public API documented in src/types.ts
claims.rationale: JSON schema serves as the stable contract for programmatic consumers
claims.excerpt: The JSON schema is stable public API documented in `src/types.ts` (`SymbolContextBundle`).

claims.claim: The compact grammar is parsed by Claude's LLM, not by a formal parser; minor formatting inconsistencies are tolerated
claims.rationale: Explains the tolerance model for compact format consumption
claims.excerpt: The compact grammar is parsed by Claude's LLM, not by a formal parser. Minor formatting inconsistencies are tolerated by the consumer

claims.claim: Adding a new config field requires bumping the version field, documenting in DESIGN.md, and adding to the ContextAtlasConfig type
claims.rationale: Ensures config changes are deliberate decisions
claims.excerpt: Adding a new config field is a deliberate decision — bumps the `version` field, documented in DESIGN.md, added to `ContextAtlasConfig` type.

claims.claim: Config file format must be YAML; JSON, TOML, and TS files are not allowed
claims.rationale: YAML is the standard for dev-tool config files (GitHub Actions, Docker, Kubernetes, CircleCI)
claims.excerpt: MUST be YAML. Not JSON, not TOML, not a TS file.

claims.claim: Config file must be a single file with no includes, imports, or inheritance chains
claims.rationale: No inheritance means no debugging of 'what config is actually active?' questions
claims.excerpt: MUST be a single file. No includes, no imports, no inheritance chains.

claims.claim: Config file must be located in the repo root, not in a subdirectory or elsewhere
claims.rationale: Keeps config location predictable and discoverable
claims.excerpt: MUST be in the repo root. Not a subdirectory, not a dotfile elsewhere.

claims.claim: Config inheritance via 'extends' from a parent config is rejected
claims.rationale: Explicitly rejected to avoid doubling the mental model
claims.excerpt: Inheritance from a parent config (`extends: ../shared.yml`)

claims.claim: Config must validate against the declared schema; unknown top-level keys are errors, not warnings
claims.rationale: Strict schema validation surfaces config bugs at startup, not at runtime when a missing key causes unexpected behavior
claims.excerpt: MUST validate against the declared schema. Unknown top-level keys are errors, not warnings.

claims.claim: Config schema has roughly seven top-level sections: language list, ADR path, doc globs, git settings, extraction model, and atlas sync options
claims.rationale: Deliberately compact config surface to stay focused
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas's config needs are limited: language list, ADR path, doc globs, git settings, extraction model, and atlas sync options. Roughly seven top-level sections.

claims.claim: Configuration must live in a single .contextatlas.yml file at the repo root
claims.rationale: A single file is greppable, copyable, and reviewable in a single PR; avoids scope creep and complexity
claims.excerpt: Configuration lives in a single `.contextatlas.yml` file at the repo root.

claims.claim: Cross-repo config references (e.g., imports-from) are rejected
claims.rationale: Explicitly rejected as out of scope for MVP
claims.excerpt: Cross-repo references (`imports-from: other-repo/.contextatlas.yml`)

claims.claim: Environment-specific config overrides are rejected
claims.rationale: Explicitly rejected to keep config surface compact
claims.excerpt: Environment-specific overrides

claims.claim: Monorepo workspace awareness is rejected
claims.rationale: Monorepo users with multiple distinct projects need multiple configs (one per project root). This is accepted.
claims.excerpt: Monorepo workspace awareness

claims.claim: Teams wanting shared config across repos must copy it or use symlinks; no framework-level inheritance is provided
claims.rationale: Consequence of rejecting inheritance and cross-repo references
claims.excerpt: Teams that want a shared config across repos must copy it, or use symlinks. No framework-level inheritance.

claims.claim: The 'version: 1' field at the top of the config is the migration handle for future breaking changes and must be preserved
claims.rationale: It is the migration handle for future breaking changes; treat it as sacred
claims.excerpt: The `version: 1` field at the top of the config is the migration handle for future breaking changes. Treat it as sacred.

claims.claim: Atlas v1.1 added extracted_at_sha and git_commits fields for target-repo git signals.
claims.rationale: Documents the additive minor bump history for the atlas schema.
claims.excerpt: v1.0 → v1.1 (ADR-11) — adds extracted_at_sha + git_commits (target-repo git signals).

claims.claim: Atlas v1.2 added symbols[].parent_id for Go interface→method relationship after flattening.
claims.rationale: Documents the additive minor bump history.
claims.excerpt: v1.1 → v1.2 (ADR-14, §Decision 4) — adds symbols[].parent_id

claims.claim: Atlas v1.3 added generator.contextatlas_commit_sha — the tool's HEAD, distinct from extracted_at_sha's target repo HEAD.
claims.rationale: Documents the additive minor bump history.
claims.excerpt: v1.2 → v1.3 (v0.3 Theme 1.3) — adds generator.contextatlas_commit_sha

claims.claim: AtlasExporter and AtlasImporter must implement lossless bidirectional sync — round-tripping a canonical atlas must be a no-op.
claims.rationale: Implementation requires bidirectional sync to maintain consistency between atlas.json and index.db.
claims.excerpt: Both must be lossless — round-tripping a canonical atlas must be a no-op.

claims.claim: CI should flag drift when ADR/code changes are made without corresponding atlas.json updates; ideally a pre-commit hook regenerates atlas.json automatically.
claims.rationale: PR workflow requires atlas updates to accompany ADR/code changes to maintain canonical state.
claims.excerpt: If a developer edits an ADR and doesn't update atlas.json, CI should flag the drift. Ideally a pre-commit hook regenerates atlas.json automatically

claims.claim: Claims in atlas.json must be sorted by (source, symbol_id, claim) and arrays produced in stable order.
claims.rationale: Stable ordering is required so git diffs are meaningful and the artifact is reviewable.
claims.excerpt: Symbols sorted by ID. Claims sorted by (source, symbol_id, claim). Arrays produced in stable order.

claims.claim: For very large repos, chunking atlas.json (e.g., one file per source ADR) may be a future enhancement to address diff review degradation.
claims.rationale: Large atlases (megabytes) degrade the diff review experience even though git handles them.
claims.excerpt: For very large repos, chunking atlas.json (one file per source ADR, for example) may be a future enhancement.

claims.claim: Future minor version bumps must add only optional fields, follow the round-trip nullish convention (empty/undefined/null collapse to omission), and leave earlier-version atlases readable without modification.
claims.rationale: The omit-when-absent rule preserves backward compatibility across minor versions; anything else is a major bump.
claims.excerpt: any future minor bump MUST add only optional fields, MUST follow the round-trip nullish convention... and MUST leave earlier-version atlases readable without modification.

claims.claim: Incremental updates must diff current file SHAs against SHAs recorded in atlas.json and re-extract only changed files.
claims.rationale: Without SHA-based change detection the system would either re-extract everything or miss updates.
claims.excerpt: Incremental updates: Diff current file SHAs against SHAs recorded in atlas.json. Re-extract only changed files.

claims.claim: On first run with a committed atlas present, the system must import atlas.json into local index.db with zero API calls.
claims.rationale: Zero onboarding cost is essential for team adoption.
claims.excerpt: First run with committed atlas present: Import atlas.json into local index.db. Zero API calls. Ready immediately.

claims.claim: Privacy: the atlas contains distilled ADR content (excerpts, claims, rationale); the default committed:true assumes ADRs are already committed and adds no new exposure.
claims.rationale: Privacy implications should be documented but not enforced — teams know their own situation.
claims.excerpt: Atlas contains distilled ADR content (excerpts, claims, rationale)... This assumption should be documented but not enforced — teams know their own privacy situation.

claims.claim: atlas.json is a committed team artifact, lives at .contextatlas/atlas.json by default, and is committed to the repo alongside source code and ADRs.
claims.rationale: Committing the atlas eliminates onboarding cost, prevents cross-developer drift, and makes architectural knowledge a first-class team artifact.
claims.excerpt: atlas.json — the committed team artifact... Lives at .contextatlas/atlas.json by default... Committed to the repo alongside source code and ADRs

claims.claim: atlas.json must be human-readable JSON with deterministic ordering (symbols and claims sorted by ID) so git diffs are focused and readable.
claims.rationale: Deterministic ordering is required to make the atlas reviewable in PRs; non-determinism creates diff noise.
claims.excerpt: Deterministic ordering (symbols and claims sorted by ID) so git diffs are focused and readable.

claims.claim: atlas.json must be self-describing, including generator version, model used, and SHAs of all source files it was built from.
claims.rationale: SHA tracking is load-bearing for incremental reindex to distinguish unchanged from changed files.
claims.excerpt: Self-describing: includes generator version, model used, and SHAs of all source files it was built from

claims.claim: atlas.json schema is a versioned public API; breaking changes require a major version bump and automatic migration (users must not regenerate by hand).
claims.rationale: atlas.json is user-visible and committed; changes are expensive and require user coordination.
claims.excerpt: Breaking changes to the atlas schema require a major version bump. Migration from v1 to v2 must be handled automatically — users should not have to regenerate atlases by hand.

claims.claim: atlas.json schema stability is stricter than the internal SQLite schema; keep it narrow enough to be stable.
claims.rationale: SQLite schema can evolve with migrations, but atlas.json changes are user-visible and expensive.
claims.excerpt: Schema stability of atlas.json is stricter than the internal SQLite schema... Keep the atlas schema narrow enough to be stable.

claims.claim: index.db is a local derived SQLite cache, lives at .contextatlas/index.db, and must be gitignored — never committed.
claims.rationale: It is a derived performance cache rebuildable from atlas.json; committing binary SQLite would make PRs unreadable.
claims.excerpt: index.db — the local derived cache... Gitignored — never committed

claims.claim: The config schema must surface the commit-or-not decision explicitly via an `atlas.committed` flag, defaulting to true.
claims.rationale: Some teams have privacy/compliance reasons to keep the atlas local-only; this must be a deliberate, supported choice.
claims.excerpt: The config schema surfaces this as a deliberate choice... committed: true # default; commits atlas.json

claims.claim: When committed: false, the system must operate correctly in a per-user extraction degraded mode as a first-class supported mode.
claims.rationale: Some teams legitimately cannot commit the atlas (regulated environments); the degraded mode must work correctly, not just partially.
claims.excerpt: The 'committed: false' escape hatch must be a first-class mode... The system must work correctly in this mode

claims.claim: A cross-call cache for getTypeInfo is post-MVP and gated on benchmark evidence.
claims.rationale: Current per-call cost (~12 LSP round-trips for a central class) is acceptable at bundle-build time without caching.
claims.excerpt: Acceptable at bundle-build time; a cross-call cache is post-MVP and gated on benchmark evidence.

claims.claim: Adapters must not throw for symbols with no type relationships; they return empty arrays.
claims.rationale: Utility functions, standalone classes, and marker interfaces legitimately have no relationships and must be handled gracefully.
claims.excerpt: Adapters MUST NOT throw for symbols that have no relationships; they return `{ extends: [], implements: [], usedByTypes: [] }`.

claims.claim: An adapter that throws 'not implemented' from getTypeInfo cannot ship.
claims.rationale: getTypeInfo is part of the plugin contract on par with listSymbols and findReferences.
claims.excerpt: An adapter that throws "not implemented" from `getTypeInfo` cannot ship.

claims.claim: Any change to TypeInfo is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.
claims.rationale: The adapter interface is a stable public contract consumed by multiple adapters and the renderer.
claims.excerpt: Any change to `TypeInfo` is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.

claims.claim: Every LanguageAdapter implementation must implement getTypeInfo(id) as a required method.
claims.rationale: Type relationships are a core capability needed by impact_of_change and get_symbol_context; defining it now before multiple adapters exist avoids retrofitting.
claims.excerpt: The `LanguageAdapter` interface defined in `src/types.ts` includes `getTypeInfo(id)` as a required method on every adapter implementation

claims.claim: Generic constraints must be excluded from extends/implements; balanced <...> spans are stripped before token extraction.
claims.rationale: Matches developer intent — `class Box<T extends Widget>` does not make Widget a parent of Box.
claims.excerpt: `class Box<T extends Widget>` does NOT register `Widget` as a parent of `Box`. The parser strips balanced `<...>` spans from the declaration header before extracting extends/implements tokens.

claims.claim: The TypeScript adapter should include a hover-format canary test in its test suite to catch tsserver hover format changes at CI time.
claims.rationale: tsserver's hover markdown has varied across major versions and could break the permissive parser.
claims.excerpt: A hover-format canary test in the adapter's test suite catches this at CI time rather than at runtime.

claims.claim: The TypeScript adapter warmup opens every project source file before returning from initialize.
claims.rationale: Ensures the LSP has analyzed enough files for usedByTypes to return correct inverse references in correctly configured workspaces.
claims.excerpt: The TypeScript adapter's warmup opens every project source file before returning from `initialize`, which is sufficient for correctly configured workspaces.

claims.claim: The compact bundle formatter (step 6) reads TypeInfo directly for deep-depth output.
claims.rationale: Changes to TypeInfo propagate to the renderer and any committed atlas fixtures.
claims.excerpt: The compact bundle formatter (step 6) reads `TypeInfo` directly for deep-depth output.

claims.claim: Type-relationship extraction lives in the language adapter, not the storage layer.
claims.rationale: Storage layer holds symbol IDs and claims with no language grammar; only the language server knows type relationships.
claims.excerpt: The storage layer holds symbol IDs and claims; it has no TypeScript or Python grammar. The language server does. This knowledge must live in the adapter.

claims.claim: TypeScript adapter extracts extends/implements from textDocument/hover and usedByTypes from textDocument/implementation.
claims.rationale: Standard LSP requests supported by typescript-language-server and Pyright provide the primitives.
claims.excerpt: `extends` and `implements` — parse from `textDocument/hover` output ... `usedByTypes` — `textDocument/implementation` at the symbol's declaration position returns the set of subclasses / implementors

claims.claim: impact_of_change requires structured inverse type data; a stubbed usedByTypes would silently under-report blast radius.
claims.rationale: Without inverse type data, impact analysis degrades to reference counts that conflate usages with subclasses.
claims.excerpt: Shipping a stubbed `usedByTypes` that always returns `[]` would produce a silently-wrong impact bundle

claims.claim: usedByTypes accuracy is conditional on LSP state; empty results should be treated as 'no known children at this moment', not an absolute claim.
claims.rationale: Language servers only return inverse references for analyzed files; crashes, excludeDirs, or warmup errors can cause under-reporting.
claims.excerpt: callers that act on `usedByTypes` should treat empty results as "no known children at this moment," not as an absolute claim.

claims.claim: usedByTypes returns direct children only; transitive closure is not returned.
claims.rationale: Matches tsserver's textDocument/implementation default behavior and prevents adapters from disagreeing on transitivity.
claims.excerpt: Direct children only — transitive closure is not returned.

claims.claim: --config <file> selects a non-default config filename; relative values resolve against configRoot, absolute values bypass configRoot for file location but paths inside the config still resolve relative to configRoot
claims.rationale: Enables benchmarks-style repos to store many configs and select one per invocation without changing path semantics
claims.excerpt: Absolute values of `--config` bypass `configRoot` for file location but do not change how paths inside that config resolve — `atlas.path` and `source.root` remain relative to `configRoot`.

claims.claim: A round-trip canary test asserts the main repo's committed atlas survives storage code changes byte-identically
claims.rationale: Catches accidental drift that would break the 'existing atlases read unchanged' promise
claims.excerpt: A round-trip canary test asserts the main repo's own committed atlas survives storage code changes byte-identically.

claims.claim: ADR files are identified by content-hash, not by position in a code tree
claims.rationale: Justifies relaxing path constraints for ADRs while keeping them for source
claims.excerpt: They were never meant to apply to ADR files, which are identified by content-hash, not by position in a code tree.

claims.claim: ADRs must be readable from the filesystem; URL-based, archive-based, or VCS-protocol-based ADR sources are out of scope
claims.rationale: Scope limitation
claims.excerpt: **Non-filesystem ADR roots.** ADRs must be readable from the filesystem.

claims.claim: Atlas format version stays at '1.0' and existing atlases must read and round-trip unchanged
claims.rationale: Backward compatibility with every existing committed atlas
claims.excerpt: Atlas format version stays at `"1.0"`. Existing atlases read and round-trip unchanged.

claims.claim: Atlases are source-root-specific; an atlas generated against source root A cannot be used against source root B — relocating ADRs across source roots requires re-extraction
claims.rationale: SHAs and path contexts change across source roots
claims.excerpt: Atlases are source-root-specific by design; relocating ADRs across source roots requires re-extraction because SHAs and path contexts change.

claims.claim: MCP runtime binary accepts --config-root <path> CLI flag; unknown flags and missing/empty values must reject with actionable errors with no silent fallback to cwd
claims.rationale: Prevents silent misconfiguration from typos
claims.excerpt: Unknown flags and missing/empty values reject with actionable errors; there's no silent fallback to cwd on typos.

claims.claim: No merge semantics are defined for combining ADR trees from different sources; aggregation callers must deduplicate before invoking ContextAtlas
claims.rationale: Colliding ADR identifiers have undefined behavior
claims.excerpt: If two ADRs have colliding identifiers (e.g., both declare `ADR-01`), behavior is undefined; aggregation callers must deduplicate before invoking ContextAtlas.

claims.claim: Only one adrs.path per config is supported; multiple ADR roots in a single config are not supported
claims.rationale: Out of scope; teams should aggregate externally or run extraction multiple times
claims.excerpt: **Multiple ADR roots in a single config.** Only one `adrs.path` per config.

claims.claim: Original pipeline coupled source location to ADR location because walkProseFiles enforced ADR paths under source root via toRelativePath
claims.rationale: Explains prior coupling that this ADR removes
claims.excerpt: `walkProseFiles` enforced that ADR absolute paths must be under the source root via `toRelativePath`, which throws when an ADR path falls outside.

claims.claim: Runtime resolution order: configRoot from --config-root or cwd, then config loaded from configRoot/.contextatlas.yml, then sourceRoot resolved from configRoot+source.root or configRoot; atlas/cache resolve against configRoot, adapters against sourceRoot
claims.rationale: Deterministic resolution sequence at runtime startup
claims.excerpt: Resolution order at runtime startup: 1. `configRoot` = `--config-root` value if passed, else `process.cwd()`.

claims.claim: Runtime source.root is declared in the config file rather than passed as a function argument, unlike extraction-side configRoot
claims.rationale: Runtime binary is spawned with only CLI args and env; pinning source location belongs in config, not spawn-time conventions
claims.excerpt: Unlike extraction-side `configRoot`, the runtime `source.root` is declared in the config file rather than passed as a function argument.

claims.claim: Source-file walking must continue to use repoRoot; only prose file walking (ADRs + docs) is affected by configRoot
claims.rationale: Preserves the source-under-source-root invariant from ADR-01 (symbol ID stability and security)
claims.excerpt: Source-file walking continues to use `repoRoot`. The source-under-source-root invariant from ADR-01 stays intact. Only prose file walking (ADRs + docs) is affected by `configRoot`.

claims.claim: adrs.path may resolve to a location outside the source root, accepting relative-under-root, absolute, or traversal paths
claims.rationale: Decouples ADR location from source location to support shared-architecture repos, benchmarks repos, and independently-versioned docs
claims.excerpt: `adrs.path` in `.contextatlas.yml` may resolve to a location outside the source root. It accepts three forms

claims.claim: adrs.path must name a directory; single-ADR-file configurations are not supported
claims.rationale: File-pointing adrs.path is unsupported; wrap in a directory
claims.excerpt: `adrs.path` must name a directory. Single-ADR-file configurations are not supported; wrap the file in a directory.

claims.claim: runExtractionPipeline accepts an optional configRoot dep that defaults to repoRoot, acting as the resolution base for adrs.path and docs.include
claims.rationale: Preserves current behavior when config lives alongside source while enabling separation when needed
claims.excerpt: The pipeline accepts a new optional `configRoot` parameter on `runExtractionPipeline`'s deps... If omitted, `configRoot` defaults to `repoRoot`, preserving current behavior

claims.claim: source_path values follow a two-branch rule: under repoRoot → stored relative to repoRoot; outside repoRoot → stored relative to ADR base directory (ADR bucket) or configRoot (docs bucket)
claims.rationale: Deterministic storage with backward compatibility for existing committed atlases
claims.excerpt: `source_path` values in `atlas.json` follow a deterministic two-branch rule

claims.claim: toRelativePath remains strict and continues to enforce 'under root' for source files
claims.rationale: Source code identity depends on canonical relative paths; relaxation is scoped to prose files only
claims.excerpt: `toRelativePath` stays strict and continues to enforce "under root" for source files. The relaxation is local to prose-file path storage

claims.claim: walkProseFiles signature gains an optional third argument for configRoot; callers passing only the first two arguments retain current behavior
claims.rationale: Backward-compatible extension
claims.excerpt: `walkProseFiles` signature gains an optional third argument for `configRoot`. Callers passing only the first two arguments retain current behavior.

claims.claim: A canary test must verify FTS row count matches claims row count after insert/delete/import operations
claims.rationale: Ensures trigger-maintained FTS index stays consistent with base table
claims.excerpt: A canary test verifies FTS row count matches `claims` row count after each of those operations.

claims.claim: A virtual table claims_fts indexes the claim, rationale, and excerpt text columns of the claims table using FTS5
claims.rationale: Substrate decision: FTS5 over LIKE because LIKE forces hand-rolled tokenization and ranking
claims.excerpt: A virtual table `claims_fts` indexes the `claim + rationale + excerpt` text columns of the `claims` table.

claims.claim: BM25 + FTS5 ranking primitives are reused by get_symbol_context's intent block per ADR-16
claims.rationale: ADR-16 delivered the extension with a different tiebreaker chain (no name-overlap)
claims.excerpt: This ADR's BM25 + FTS5 ranking primitives are reused by `get_symbol_context`'s intent block when an optional caller-provided query is present.

claims.claim: Compact output uses the SYM/SIG/INTENT vocabulary shared with get_symbol_context
claims.rationale: Unified vocabulary lets LLMs parse output without re-learning
claims.excerpt: Compact output shape (matches `get_symbol_context`'s vocabulary — `SYM`, `SIG`, `INTENT`

claims.claim: Each match surfaces only the top-scoring claim per symbol even if multiple claims match
claims.rationale: A symbol may match via multiple claims; the response carries one matched_intent
claims.excerpt: a symbol may be matched via multiple claims, we surface the top-scoring one

claims.claim: Embedding-based semantic search is explicitly deferred and evidence-gated via benchmarks
claims.rationale: Per DESIGN.md, not speculative; only with benchmark evidence
claims.excerpt: **Embedding-based semantic search.** Explicitly deferred per DESIGN.md. Evidence-gated via benchmarks, not speculative.

claims.claim: Empty query after sanitization returns MATCHES 0 with a hint, not an error
claims.rationale: Matches the primitive's render-cleanly pattern for empty input
claims.excerpt: Empty query after sanitization (e.g. `"!!!"` → ""): tool returns `MATCHES 0` with a hint in the compact text, not an error.

claims.claim: FTS index is maintained by triggers mirroring INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on claims, not by a separate FTS-specific import path
claims.rationale: Keeps atlas round-trip lossless and avoids changes to insertClaim/deleteClaimsBySourcePath/importAtlas
claims.excerpt: The FTS index is maintained by triggers that mirror `INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE` on `claims`.

claims.claim: Input schema is restricted to query, limit, and format; severity_filter and source_filter are post-MVP
claims.rationale: Faceted search is a natural extension but deferred
claims.excerpt: Input today is `query` + `limit` + `format`.

claims.claim: MATCH string is constructed as exact phrase OR individual word tokens to enable BM25 phrase-vs-scatter weighting in one query
claims.rationale: Single MATCH string lets BM25 do phrase weighting; manual two-pass merging duplicates scorer logic
claims.excerpt: "<sanitized exact phrase>" OR word1 OR word2 OR word3

claims.claim: Name-overlap tiebreaker is required to disambiguate ADR-08 frontmatter-hint siblings sharing the same claim
claims.rationale: ADR-08 auto-links a claim to multiple symbols; without name-overlap, the namesake symbol may not rank first
claims.excerpt: the original three-level tiebreaker chain couldn't distinguish them, so query `normalizePath` returned arbitrary ordering where name-match should rank first

claims.claim: Queries must be sanitized (non-alphanumeric stripped to spaces) before being passed to FTS5 MATCH
claims.rationale: Users cannot pass FTS5 MATCH syntax directly; advanced query DSL is post-MVP
claims.excerpt: Queries are sanitized (non-alphanumeric stripped to spaces) and passed to FTS5 via the `MATCH` operator

claims.claim: Raw FTS5 MATCH syntax is not exposed; all input is sanitized and MATCH construction is internal
claims.rationale: User input is natural language; raw_query escape hatch is post-MVP only if real use cases appear
claims.excerpt: All input sanitized; the MATCH string construction is internal.

claims.claim: Response defaults to compact text format with JSON opt-in via a format input parameter
claims.rationale: Mirrors get_symbol_context's convention so consumers pick format once across tools
claims.excerpt: Mirrors the primitive's convention: compact text by default, JSON opt-in via a `format` input parameter.

claims.claim: Response must not include a numeric relevance score; rank is implicit in array order
claims.rationale: BM25 scores aren't calibrated across queries; surfacing them reads as false probability/precision
claims.excerpt: Deliberately **no `relevance` score** in the response. BM25 scores aren't calibrated across queries

claims.claim: Results must be ordered by bm25(claims_fts) ascending (more negative = more relevant) as primary sort
claims.rationale: FTS5's bm25 returns negative values where more-negative is more relevant
claims.excerpt: The query `ORDER BY bm25(claims_fts) ASC LIMIT ?` gives best-first truncated to the requested page.

claims.claim: Schema migration v2 adds the FTS5 virtual table and triggers via the existing migration runner
claims.rationale: Existing migration runner extends cleanly with one new entry, no refactor
claims.excerpt: New schema migration v2. The existing migration runner from step 3 extends cleanly — one new entry in `MIGRATIONS`, no refactor.

claims.claim: Severity ordering hard > soft > context matches buildBundle's primitive intent ordering for cross-tool consistency
claims.rationale: Callers used to that ordering get consistent treatment across tools
claims.excerpt: **Severity:** `hard > soft > context`. Matches the primitive's intent ordering in `buildBundle`

claims.claim: Source field is not indexed in FTS; filtering by source is post-MVP
claims.rationale: Source is identifier-shaped, not a search axis; identifier-shaped fields produce noise in natural-language FTS
claims.excerpt: Source is **not** indexed in FTS. It's an identifier-shaped filter ("ADR-07"), not a search axis.

claims.claim: Tiebreaker chain after BM25: name-overlap descending, severity (hard>soft>context), source alphabetical, claim id ascending
claims.rationale: Deterministic, reviewable ordering consistent with buildBundle's intent ordering
claims.excerpt: Full sort chain (primary score first, then tiebreakers in order).

claims.claim: Tokenizer is FTS5's default unicode61 (lowercase, split on non-letter-or-digit); no custom tokenizer in v0.1
claims.rationale: Empirical dogfood verified unicode61 handles natural-language claim content adequately
claims.excerpt: FTS5's default `unicode61` tokenizer lowercases and splits on non-letter-or-digit characters.

claims.claim: Zero matches render as 'MATCHES 0 [query="..."]' with no body lines, consistent with the omit-empty-sections rule
claims.rationale: Consistent with primitive's render-cleanly-when-empty pattern
claims.excerpt: Zero matches render as `MATCHES 0 [query="..."]` with no body lines — consistent with the primitive's "omit empty sections" rule.

claims.claim: atlas.json contains claims only; FTS is a derived local index
claims.rationale: Preserves lossless atlas round-trip
claims.excerpt: Atlas round-trip stays lossless: `atlas.json` contains claims only; FTS is a derived local index.

claims.claim: find_by_intent does not perform cross-claim reasoning or synthesize across multiple ADRs
claims.rationale: Returns symbols whose single claim best matches; composition is the caller's job via get_symbol_context
claims.excerpt: `find_by_intent` returns symbols whose single claim best matches the query; it doesn't compose claims across multiple ADRs into a synthesized answer.

claims.claim: find_by_intent must be a thin composite over the claims table, not a new infrastructure layer
claims.rationale: Per DESIGN.md, this tool is composed over existing claims storage rather than introducing a new layer
claims.excerpt: Per DESIGN.md this is a thin composite over the claims table — not a new infrastructure layer.

claims.claim: find_by_intent must not make query-time Anthropic API calls, use embeddings, or LLM re-ranking
claims.rationale: ADR-02 forbids Anthropic API calls outside the extraction pipeline; query-time purity is required
claims.excerpt: ADR-02 forbids Anthropic API calls outside the extraction pipeline. This tool's ranking is 100% SQL + FTS5 BM25, executed locally

claims.claim: find_by_intent searches claims, not symbol metadata; callers who know the symbol name should use get_symbol_context
claims.rationale: Tool scope is claims search; symbol-name lookup is a separate tool
claims.excerpt: Since `find_by_intent` is a claims search, not a symbol-name search, a user who knows the symbol name by literal form should use `get_symbol_context` directly.

claims.claim: insertClaim, deleteClaimsBySourcePath, and importAtlas do not require code changes; FTS sync is automatic via triggers
claims.rationale: Triggers keep FTS in sync; a canary test verifies row-count parity
claims.excerpt: `insertClaim` / `deleteClaimsBySourcePath` / `importAtlas` do not need changes — the triggers keep FTS in sync automatically.

claims.claim: limit input defaults to 5, max 50; no offset or cursor pagination in MVP
claims.rationale: Single-page semantics keep response bounded and composable
claims.excerpt: `limit` input, default 5, max 50. Callers wanting more narrow their query.

claims.claim: --check does not start the MCP server or adapters; it is a short-circuit mode that exits before MCP setup.
claims.rationale: Cheap metadata read for CI and hooks.
claims.excerpt: `--check` does not start the MCP server or the adapters; it's a cheap metadata read.

claims.claim: A file is hot when it appears in ≥ config.git.recentCommits distinct commits within the 500-commit window.
claims.rationale: One knob, one meaning — reuses existing config field.
claims.excerpt: A file is **hot** when it appears in `≥ config.git.recentCommits` distinct commits within the 500-commit extraction window.

claims.claim: Atlas stores commits with embedded files arrays; SQLite git_file_commits is derived on import by flattening.
claims.rationale: Keeps atlas compact while letting SQL do the file-pivoted lookups.
claims.excerpt: Atlas stores `git_commits` with embedded `files` arrays; the local cache **derives** `git_file_commits` on import by flattening those arrays.

claims.claim: Attribution is file-level only, via one-hop symbol.path; no line-range blame or hunk heuristics.
claims.rationale: Line-level blame is expensive, unstable under formatting churn, and wrong under refactors.
claims.excerpt: Git operates on files. The symbol → git mapping is **one-hop via `symbol.path`**... No line-range blame, no hunks-that-intersect-signature heuristics.

claims.claim: ContextAtlas ships staleness primitives but does not prescribe an update workflow.
claims.rationale: Teams compose their own policy from --check, re-extraction, and commit steps.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas ships the primitives (`--check`, re-run extraction, commit the regenerated atlas) and deliberately does not prescribe a workflow.

claims.claim: DEFAULT_SIGNALS adds 'git'; buildBundle populates the git block when the signal is included.
claims.rationale: Primitive's 'return everything in one call' philosophy covers every declared axis.
claims.excerpt: `DEFAULT_SIGNALS` adds `"git"` — the primitive's "return everything in one call" philosophy finally covers every declared axis.

claims.claim: Extraction records current git HEAD SHA in atlas.extracted_at_sha; field is optional on read, always written on extract.
claims.rationale: Enables staleness detection while preserving backward compatibility with pre-1.1 atlases.
claims.excerpt: The field is optional on read so pre-1.1 atlases load unchanged; it is always written on extract.

claims.claim: Extraction runs `git log -500 --no-merges --name-only` against repoRoot; merge commits are dropped.
claims.rationale: Merge commits inflate co-change without representing real edits.
claims.excerpt: Merge commits are dropped — they inflate co-change without representing real edits.

claims.claim: Git always runs against repoRoot, never configRoot.
claims.rationale: Source paths and source-file git history live together; ADRs and docs live wherever the author put them (ADR-08 symmetry).
claims.excerpt: Git always runs against **`repoRoot`**, never `configRoot`.

claims.claim: Git data is extracted at index time and served from storage; no MCP tool shells out to git at query time.
claims.rationale: Query-time git would spawn subprocesses per call and re-parse unchanged history; index-time gives cheap deterministic reads.
claims.excerpt: Git data is extracted during the extraction pipeline (step 5 of the build order, now bearing a git phase) and lives in SQLite + atlas.json. No MCP tool shells out to `git` at query time.

claims.claim: Hotness uses commit-count against the extraction window, not elapsed-days or author-breadth.
claims.rationale: Elapsed-days reintroduces clock dependency at query time; author-breadth is post-MVP.
claims.excerpt: **Elapsed-days threshold** (e.g. "touched in last 30 days") — rejected because it re-introduces a clock dependency at query time.

claims.claim: If repoRoot is not inside a git working tree, the git phase logs a warning, records zero commits, and the pipeline completes without hard failure.
claims.rationale: Git absence is a legitimate state (fresh checkout, tarball deployments) that shouldn't block extraction.
claims.excerpt: No hard failure — git absence is a legitimate state (fresh checkout not yet initialized, tarball deployments) that shouldn't block extraction.

claims.claim: Line-level blame and hunk-intersection symbol attribution are non-goals.
claims.rationale: File-level attribution is the final granularity; refinement is post-MVP and evidence-gated.
claims.excerpt: **Line-level blame or hunk-intersection symbol attribution.** File-level is the attribution granularity.

claims.claim: No flag chains --check with auto-extraction in v0.1; teams script staleness policy themselves.
claims.rationale: Teams decide whether staleness is blocking in their own workflow.
claims.excerpt: **Forced re-indexing on staleness.** `--check` exits non-zero when stale; no flag chains the check with auto-extraction in v0.1.

claims.claim: No pre-computed git_cochange cache table is shipped for v0.1; co-change is computed on-the-fly via self-join.
claims.rationale: Self-join is fast on 500-commit windows; a cache adds invalidation complexity without measured need.
claims.excerpt: A pre-computed `git_cochange` cache table is deliberately **not** shipped for v0.1 — it's a post-MVP optimization if benchmark evidence shows the self-join dominates query latency.

claims.claim: Non-git VCS backends (hg, fossil, svn) are out of scope.
claims.rationale: Other VCS support would require a separate ADR with its own extraction path.
claims.excerpt: **Non-`git` VCS backends.** `hg`, `fossil`, `svn` are out.

claims.claim: Per-symbol commit filtering via semantic matching on commit messages is out of scope; impact_of_change uses only file-level co-change.
claims.rationale: Message-mining is a separate feature pending evidence.
claims.excerpt: `impact_of_change` uses file-level co-change, not "commits whose message mentions this symbol." Message-mining is a separate feature if evidence ever asks.

claims.claim: Prose extraction remains SHA-incremental because Opus-backed stages are expensive.
claims.rationale: Explains why the unchanged-skip optimization applies only to expensive stages.
claims.excerpt: Prose extraction remains SHA-incremental per DESIGN.md — only Opus-backed stages pay the unchanged-skip optimization because they're the expensive stages.

claims.claim: Query-time git log calls are a hard boundary; fresh git data requires re-running extraction.
claims.rationale: Enforces index-time discipline.
claims.excerpt: **Query-time `git log` calls.** Hard boundary. If a query needs fresh git data, the user re-runs extraction.

claims.claim: Schema migration v3 is additive — no touches to v1 or v2 tables.
claims.rationale: Existing atlases read into v1/v2 state; v3 tables start empty.
claims.excerpt: Schema migration v3 is additive — no touches to v1 or v2 tables.

claims.claim: Staleness is advisory, not blocking — a stale atlas still serves queries.
claims.rationale: Teams have legitimate reasons to run slightly-stale atlases (benchmarks, debugging, API-key scarcity).
claims.excerpt: **Staleness is advisory, not blocking.** A stale atlas still serves queries.

claims.claim: Storage layer must accept both v1.0 and v1.1 atlases on read; every fresh extraction writes v1.1.
claims.rationale: Backward compatibility for pre-1.1 atlases.
claims.excerpt: The storage layer accepts both — reading a v1.0 atlas still works; it just lacks the `git_commits` / `extracted_at_sha` fields. Every fresh extraction writes v1.1.

claims.claim: The --check CLI flag exits 0 when current, 1 when differing, 2 when no extracted_at_sha or not a git tree; exit code is the whole contract.
claims.rationale: No dependency on stdout parsing keeps the contract simple for CI/hooks.
claims.excerpt: The exit code is the whole contract — no dependency on stdout parsing.

claims.claim: The extraction window is capped at 500 commits per extraction (not per-symbol).
claims.rationale: Prevents unbounded growth on long-lived repos while covering multi-quarter history.
claims.excerpt: The 500-commit window is per-extraction, not per-symbol... The cap prevents unbounded growth on long-lived repos while still covering multi-quarter history for typical projects.

claims.claim: The git phase always rebuilds git_commits and git_file_commits from fresh git log on every extraction run; no SHA-incremental updates.
claims.rationale: git log is subprocess-fast; full rebuild side-steps force-push/rebase edge cases.
claims.excerpt: The git phase always rebuilds `git_commits` + `git_file_commits` from a fresh `git log` on every extraction run. No incremental merge logic, no partial updates.

claims.claim: Unbounded-history ingestion is a non-goal; 500-commit cap is fixed for v0.1.
claims.rationale: Configurable maximum is post-MVP.
claims.excerpt: **Unbounded-history ingestion.** 500 commits is the cap. Configurable maximum is post-MVP.

claims.claim: atlas.json version bumps to 1.1 and adds git_commits and extracted_at_sha fields; round-trip must be byte-identical.
claims.rationale: Preserves ADR-06 lossless round-trip discipline.
claims.excerpt: Storage is lossless-round-trip per ADR-06: `atlas.json → SQLite → atlas.json` produces a byte-identical file.

claims.claim: get_symbol_context's git block must match the shape declared by SymbolContextBundle in src/types.ts.
claims.rationale: Response shape consistency with existing type declarations.
claims.excerpt: Shape matches the `git` field already declared in `src/types.ts`'s `SymbolContextBundle`.

claims.claim: git_commits array is sorted by date descending; files within each commit sorted ascending.
claims.rationale: Deterministic ordering keeps diffs reviewable.
claims.excerpt: `git_commits` is sorted by `date` descending (newest first); `files` within each commit is sorted ascending. Deterministic ordering keeps diffs reviewable.

claims.claim: impact_of_change output includes GIT_COCHANGE (top 5) and RISK_SIGNALS blocks in the specified compact format.
claims.rationale: Format is load-bearing for step-11 tests; drift requires an ADR amendment.
claims.excerpt: The compact IMPACT block format above is load-bearing for step 11 tests; any drift requires an ADR amendment.

claims.claim: impact_of_change supports a compact default output and JSON via the existing format input parameter per ADR-09.
claims.rationale: Consistent with ADR-09 format pattern.
claims.excerpt: Compact by default; JSON available via the existing `format` input parameter (ADR-09 pattern).

claims.claim: runExtractionPipeline gains a git phase between source indexing and claim writing, and the phase must be idempotent.
claims.rationale: Running on up-to-date atlas must produce byte-identical tables.
claims.excerpt: The phase is idempotent — running it on an up-to-date atlas re-walks the same 500 commits and produces byte-identical tables.

claims.claim: A new module `src/extraction/cli-runner.ts` wraps `runExtractionPipeline` with CLI-specific concerns (API key discovery, Anthropic client construction, adapter lifecycle, summary printing, exit-code mapping).
claims.rationale: Separates CLI concerns from the library function used by tests.
claims.excerpt: New module `src/extraction/cli-runner.ts` wraps `runExtractionPipeline` with the CLI-specific concerns

claims.claim: Backward compatibility: any argv accepted by `df39f7a` must still work, including error messages on malformed input. Subcommand parsing is additive.
claims.rationale: `--config-root`, `--config`, and `--check` are shipped contracts.
claims.excerpt: Any argv that worked against `df39f7a` must still work, including error messages on malformed input.

claims.claim: Before this ADR, the extraction pipeline existed only as a library function called from tests, with no user-facing way to refresh an atlas.
claims.rationale: Motivates the ADR.
claims.excerpt: The extraction pipeline (`runExtractionPipeline`) exists as a library function called only from tests. There is no user-facing way to refresh an atlas.

claims.claim: Cost is computed under Opus 4.7 pricing constants ($15/M input, $75/M output) defined in `src/extraction/pricing.ts`; failed-retry token usage is not captured.
claims.rationale: Visibility limited to responses actually received.
claims.excerpt: constants in `src/extraction/pricing.ts`... Failed-retry token usage is not captured — visibility is limited to responses we actually received.

claims.claim: Each subcommand defines its own 0/1/2 exit code semantics; there is no unified exit code scheme.
claims.rationale: Unifying would break ADR-11's `--check` 0/1/2 contract; matches POSIX tools like git, npm, kubectl.
claims.excerpt: Each subcommand defines its own 0/1/2 meanings... The rejected alternative was a unified scheme... That would break ADR-11's `--check` 0/1/2 contract

claims.claim: Flags are orthogonal modifiers that compose across operations; subcommands partition operation space with distinct argument shapes, outputs, stdio contracts, and exit-code semantics.
claims.rationale: Mixing the two patterns produces ambiguous contracts; the rule prevents drift as new operations land.
claims.excerpt: Flags are orthogonal modifiers that work across operations... Subcommands are distinct operations with distinct argument shapes, distinct outputs, distinct stdio contracts, and distinct exit-code semantics.

claims.claim: Future operations (claim capture, docstring import, PR mining) must be added as subcommands, not flags.
claims.rationale: Flags stay reserved for modifiers that apply across operations.
claims.excerpt: Future operations (claim capture from agent sessions per v0.6+, docstring import per v0.3, PR mining per v0.3, etc.) will be subcommands. Flags stay reserved for modifiers that apply across operations.

claims.claim: In `--json` output, `cost_usd` must be emitted as a number truncated to four decimals, not a string.
claims.rationale: Machine-friendly typing in JSON.
claims.excerpt: In `--json`, `cost_usd` is a number truncated to four decimals.

claims.claim: Invoking the binary with no subcommand must start the MCP server on stdio; this no-args contract cannot be broken without a major version bump.
claims.rationale: MCP clients (Claude Code, Claude Desktop) spawn the binary with no args and expect stdio JSON-RPC; it is a hard external contract under the one-way-migrations principle.
claims.excerpt: No-subcommand default is MCP serving... That is a hard external contract — Claude Code, Claude Desktop, and any other compliant client depend on it.

claims.claim: Missing `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` must produce an actionable error and exit code 2 before any side-effect-bearing work begins; no `.env` loading is supported in v0.1.
claims.rationale: Avoids cryptic SDK-level 401s after the pipeline has started; keeps runtime deps minimal.
claims.excerpt: Missing `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` produces an actionable error message... and exits code 2. Not a cryptic SDK-level 401 surfaced after the pipeline has already started walking files.

claims.claim: No general-purpose CLI framework (commander, yargs, oclif) is adopted; parsing remains hand-rolled in `cli-args.ts`.
claims.rationale: Surface is small enough that a framework would be overkill.
claims.excerpt: No adoption of commander, yargs, or oclif. Hand-rolled parsing in `cli-args.ts` continues

claims.claim: No plugin system for third-party subcommands; the subcommand table is in-tree.
claims.rationale: External-author subcommands raise architectural questions (symbol ID/atlas schema stability) deferred to v1.x.
claims.excerpt: Plugin system for third-party subcommands. The subcommand table is in-tree.

claims.claim: On atlas presence, `index` runs SHA-diff-gated incremental extraction; the git phase always runs in full per ADR-11.
claims.rationale: Defines incremental behavior path.
claims.excerpt: diff current prose SHAs against the baseline, extract only changed/added files... The git phase always runs in full (per ADR-11).

claims.claim: Output must always be non-interactive: no terminal detection, progress bars, or colored output.
claims.rationale: The binary is scripted by both MCP clients and CI systems.
claims.excerpt: Output is always non-interactive. Terminal detection, progress bars, colored output — all out of scope.

claims.claim: Passing `--check` alongside `index` must be rejected with an actionable error.
claims.rationale: `--check` belongs to the no-subcommand mode (staleness probe).
claims.excerpt: `--check` — flag belongs to the no-subcommand mode (staleness probe); passing it alongside `index` is rejected with an actionable error

claims.claim: Subcommand aliases (`reindex`, `extract`, `refresh`, `build`) are not supported; one canonical name per operation, with an actionable error pointing users to the right name.
claims.rationale: Aliases proliferate and drift.
claims.excerpt: One canonical name per operation; aliases proliferate and drift.

claims.claim: Subcommand detection must inspect the first positional user argument before flag parsing; flags may appear before or after the subcommand name.
claims.rationale: Keeps dispatch positional but allows flags to be order-insensitive relative to the subcommand.
claims.excerpt: Subcommand is the first positional argument. Before any flag parsing, the parser inspects argv[0]... subcommand detection is positional, not order-sensitive for flags.

claims.claim: The `index` subcommand must exit on completion: no lingering stdio channel, no waiting for input, no MCP-server code paths loaded; resources released before exit.
claims.rationale: Distinct lifecycle from the MCP serve path.
claims.excerpt: Exit on completion. The `index` subcommand runs the pipeline, prints the summary, and exits.

claims.claim: The `index` subcommand must never write outside the configured atlas paths.
claims.rationale: Side-effect containment.
claims.excerpt: Never writes outside the configured atlas paths

claims.claim: The `index` subcommand must shut down adapter subprocesses cleanly on exit (success, failure, or signal) via `.shutdown()` in a `finally` block — no zombie processes.
claims.rationale: Matches the shutdown discipline already in place for the MCP path.
claims.excerpt: No zombie processes. This matches the shutdown discipline already in `src/index.ts` for the MCP path: adapter `.shutdown()` called in a `finally` block.

claims.claim: The budget-warn threshold is advisory only: at most one warning fires per run on stderr, the run continues, and the exit code is unaffected.
claims.rationale: A hard cap is a distinct feature with different UX requirements; v0.2 keeps the semantic intentionally loose.
claims.excerpt: a single warning is logged to stderr... and no further warnings fire for the remainder of the run. This is advisory, not a hard cap: the run continues and the exit code is unaffected.

claims.claim: The pipeline operation is named `index`, covering walk, extract, resolve, store, export for both first-run and update cases.
claims.rationale: `extract` is too narrow, `reindex`/`refresh` presume existing state, `build` is too generic; README already documents `index`.
claims.excerpt: `index` names the complete pipeline operation: walk, extract, resolve, store, export.

claims.claim: The subcommand dispatcher must run before `loadConfig`.
claims.rationale: Different subcommands have different config-error semantics; loading config before dispatch would impose one subcommand's error model on all of them.
claims.excerpt: Dispatch before config load. The subcommand dispatcher runs before `loadConfig`.

claims.claim: There must be no separate `init` subcommand; first-run, incremental, and `--full` rebuilds all run through `contextatlas index`.
claims.rationale: Initialization is not a distinct operation — it's the no-atlas-yet case of indexing; pipeline internals branch on atlas presence.
claims.excerpt: One command, three paths. No separate `contextatlas init` subcommand — "initialize" is not a distinct operation; it's the no-atlas-yet case of indexing.

claims.claim: When all tokens resolve, `--verbose` must produce no stderr output (silent zero-unresolved case).
claims.rationale: Default summary already confirms success; cheerleader lines add noise without signal.
claims.excerpt: Zero-unresolved case: silent. No stderr emission when every token resolves.

claims.claim: `--check` remains a flag (not a subcommand), grandfathered from ADR-11; future check-like probes must be subcommands.
claims.rationale: ADR-11's `--check` 0/1/2 contract is already external and one-way-migration-locked; the asymmetry is bounded to this one case.
claims.excerpt: Flag-status is grandfathered for `--check`, not a pattern.

claims.claim: `--full` bypasses SHA-diff gating, re-extracting every prose file regardless of baseline SHA.
claims.rationale: Used for rebuilds after prompt or model changes; same cost as first-run.
claims.excerpt: `--full` flag passed. Bypass the SHA-diff gate; every prose file is re-extracted regardless of its baseline SHA.

claims.claim: `--json` output emits a single JSON object with the same fields as `key=value`; new fields may appear, existing fields never rename. Exit codes are unchanged between default and `--json` output.
claims.rationale: Same stability contract as plaintext stdout; consistent with the `format` pattern used elsewhere.
claims.excerpt: Same stability contract: new fields may appear, existing fields never rename. Exit codes are unchanged between default and `--json` output.

claims.claim: `--verbose` writes diagnostic detail to stderr only; stdout remains pinned to the stable `key=value`/`--json` contract.
claims.rationale: Follows npm/git/curl convention; preserves stdout contract for CI consumers.
claims.excerpt: Channel: stderr... stdout stays pinned to the stable ADR-12 `key=value` (or `--json`) contract.

claims.claim: `ParsedArgs` gains a `subcommand: "mcp" | "index"` field; the no-subcommand path is `mcp`.
claims.rationale: Implementation consequence of subcommand dispatch.
claims.excerpt: `ParsedArgs` gains `subcommand: "mcp" | "index"` (mcp = no subcommand = default).

claims.claim: `contextatlas index` runs in-process and exits; no daemon mode or remote execution.
claims.rationale: Long-running extraction service is a different product.
claims.excerpt: `contextatlas index` runs the pipeline in-process and exits. A long-running daemon... is a different product

claims.claim: `index` stdout must emit `key=value` summary lines by default; new keys may be added but existing keys must never be renamed.
claims.rationale: CI consumers depend on a stable parseable contract; one-way-migrations principle applies.
claims.excerpt: `key=value` lines are stable across releases; new keys may be added, existing keys never renamed.

claims.claim: `index` writes `atlas.json` only when `atlas.committed: true` and changes occurred; `atlas.local_cache` (SQLite) is always written.
claims.rationale: Defines side effects of indexing.
claims.excerpt: Writes `atlas.json` when `atlas.committed: true` and changes occurred / Writes to `atlas.local_cache` (SQLite) always

claims.claim: Single-symbol operations (listSymbols, getSymbolDetails, findReferences, getDiagnostics) must remain single-pass and not depend on the Protocol cache.
claims.rationale: Keeps query latency proportional to single-symbol work, not repo size.
claims.excerpt: **Single-symbol operations (`listSymbols`, `getSymbolDetails`, `findReferences`, `getDiagnostics`) stay single-pass.**

claims.claim: Full indexing must use a two-pass strategy: pass-1 computes each class's isProtocol flag, pass-2 computes extends/implements/usedByTypes using the cache.
claims.rationale: Pass-2 needs Protocol knowledge to route bases correctly and identify subclass candidates.
claims.excerpt: **Pass 1** (full-indexing): walk every project `.py` file, compute each class's `isProtocol` flag

claims.claim: Single-file getTypeInfo queries without inventory context must return [] for usedByTypes since textDocument/implementation is unimplemented in Pyright.
claims.rationale: Pyright returns JSON-RPC error -32601 for textDocument/implementation; usedByTypes requires full-indexing pass.
claims.excerpt: Single-file queries that don't have inventory context return `[]` for `usedByTypes`.

claims.claim: When the Protocol cache is unavailable, getTypeInfo falls back to mechanisms (a)+(b) only; project-defined Protocols may route to extends until the cache is built.
claims.rationale: Degraded but not incorrect — leaving a Protocol in extends is the less-lossy error direction per ADR-07.
claims.excerpt: The adapter falls back to mechanisms (a) + (b) only — which covers the canonical-Protocol case.

claims.claim: jedi-language-server and pylsp are rejected because they cannot satisfy ADR-07's getTypeInfo contract (no reliable cross-file type inference, no inverse-lookup).
claims.rationale: Capability disqualifier for the load-bearing getTypeInfo contract.
claims.excerpt: Rejected as a hard disqualifier: lacks reliable type-inference for `textDocument/references` across files, and has no inverse-lookup story for type relationships.

claims.claim: src/adapters/lsp-client.ts is shared unchanged between TypeScript and Python adapters; the probe validated it drives Pyright correctly.
claims.rationale: ADR-03's plugin interface holds; both adapters use the same client.
claims.excerpt: **Shared `LspClient`.** `src/adapters/lsp-client.ts` unchanged.

claims.claim: @runtime_checkable Protocols still route to implements; the decorator does not change semantic classification.
claims.rationale: The decorator only adds isinstance() support; the class is still a Protocol.
claims.excerpt: A runtime-checkable Protocol is still a Protocol; its bases still go to `implements`.

claims.claim: A shared src/adapters/conformance.ts spec plus per-adapter conformance.test.ts files lands in the same step to prove both adapters satisfy the same behavioral contract.
claims.rationale: Adapter-infrastructure decision noted in Step 9 plan, not Python-specific.
claims.excerpt: A shared `src/adapters/conformance.ts` spec plus per-adapter `conformance.test.ts` files proves both adapters satisfy the same behavioral contract.

claims.claim: ABC vs concrete-class distinction is not surfaced as a separate SymbolKind; both get 'class'.
claims.rationale: ABCs are classes; making them a separate kind requires propagating 'abstract' through the type system, out of v0.1 scope.
claims.excerpt: **ABC vs. concrete-class distinction not surfaced as separate SymbolKind.** Both get `class`.

claims.claim: Adapter must remap LSP documentSymbol kinds based on source-line inspection and hover prefix (Protocol → interface, hover '(type)' → type, etc.).
claims.rationale: Pyright reports all classes as kind 5 and all methods as kind 6, so additional disambiguation is needed.
claims.excerpt: Adapter remaps based on source-line inspection + hover prefix: Protocol bases detected in declaration header rewrite kind to `interface`

claims.claim: Adapter must walk the project root during initialize and open every .py file via didOpen to enable cross-file references.
claims.rationale: Pyright performs no automatic cross-file analysis; findReferences only returns hits in files seen via didOpen.
claims.excerpt: Adapter walks the project root during `initialize` and opens every `.py` file, mirroring TypeScriptAdapter's tsserver warmup.

claims.claim: Adapter targets Python 3.12+ (PEP 695 'type' statement supported); older Python code parses without special-case paths.
claims.rationale: No version-specific behavior paths in v0.1.
claims.excerpt: Adapter targets Python 3.12+ (PEP 695 `type` statement supported). Older Python code parses and indexes fine; no special-case paths.

claims.claim: Class signatures must be constructed from source declaration parsing, not from hover text, since Pyright hover omits class headers and bases.
claims.rationale: Pyright hover returns compact markdown without class declaration headers or base classes.
claims.excerpt: Class signatures constructed from source declaration instead (`class Widget(Shape, Serializable):`).

claims.claim: Decorators (@property, @classmethod, @staticmethod, @dataclass, @runtime_checkable) appear in the signature string but do not influence SymbolKind.
claims.rationale: Avoids commitment to caller interpretation without benchmark evidence.
claims.excerpt: appear in the signature string when present (hover already includes them) but don't influence kind.

claims.claim: Dotted base names (e.g., typing.Protocol, collections.abc.Mapping) must be preserved intact as single tokens — no further splitting on '.'.
claims.rationale: Protocol detection relies on explicit string matching against fully qualified names.
claims.excerpt: Dotted names (`typing.Protocol`, `collections.abc.Mapping`) are preserved intact as single tokens — no further splitting on `.`.

claims.claim: For overloaded functions, v0.1 surfaces one symbol per overloaded name with the first overload's signature.
claims.rationale: Pyright collapses @overload declarations into a single documentSymbol entry; full overload set is not visible at atlas layer.
claims.excerpt: v0.1 surfaces one symbol per overloaded name with the first overload's signature.

claims.claim: Import alias tracing for Protocol detection must be localized to the file's top-level imports; no whole-module import graph walk.
claims.rationale: Bounds parser scope and avoids transitive resolution complexity.
claims.excerpt: Parsing is localized to the file's top-level imports; no whole-module import graph walk.

claims.claim: Jupyter notebook (.ipynb) indexing and async/await separate-kind extraction are post-MVP non-goals.
claims.rationale: Scope boundaries for v0.1; revisit only if evidence emerges.
claims.excerpt: **Jupyter notebook indexing.** `.ipynb` is not in the extensions list

claims.claim: Parameters and instance variables (LSP kind 13 nested under a method) must not be surfaced as top-level symbols.
claims.rationale: Defines symbol granularity boundaries for the adapter.
claims.excerpt: LSP kind 13 nested under a method — not surfaced as a top-level symbol

claims.claim: Pathological declaration inputs must degrade gracefully to 'bases not extracted' rather than producing wrong extraction or raising.
claims.rationale: Mirrors TypeScriptAdapter's declaration-header discipline; preserves correctness over completeness.
claims.excerpt: Pathological cases degrade gracefully — "bases not extracted" rather than wrong extraction

claims.claim: Protocol bases must route to TypeInfo.implements; all other bases (including abc.ABC and ABC-derived classes) route to TypeInfo.extends.
