claims.rationale: Preserves Python's structural-vs-nominal distinction at the contract layer and matches TypeScript adapter semantics for cross-language consistency.
claims.excerpt: the adapter surfaces the distinction by routing Protocol bases to `implements` and everything else to `extends`

claims.claim: Protocol detection must compose three mechanisms: (a) hardcoded canonical names Protocol/typing.Protocol, (b) per-file import alias tracing, and (c) symbol-inventory lookup for project-defined Protocols.
claims.rationale: No single mechanism covers all cases; a base is treated as Protocol if any mechanism fires.
claims.excerpt: at lookup time, a base name is treated as a Protocol if ANY of the three mechanisms fires.

claims.claim: Pyright 1.1.409+ is the sole Python LSP backend for v0.1, listed as non-optional in peerDependencies and devDependencies.
claims.rationale: Mirrors the typescript-language-server pattern from ADR-03 and ratifies DESIGN.md's choice; alternatives were evaluated and rejected.
claims.excerpt: **Pyright 1.1.409+** is the sole Python LSP backend for v0.1. Listed in `peerDependencies` + `devDependencies` as non-optional

claims.claim: The adapter's extensions list is ['.py'] only; .pyi stub files are not indexed in v0.1.
claims.rationale: Stubs would double symbol count for libraries shipping both, with no consumer-visible way to disambiguate; benchmark target ships source.
claims.excerpt: The adapter's `extensions` list is `[".py"]`. **Stub files (`.pyi`) are not indexed in v0.1.**

claims.claim: The class-header parser must locate the declaration via documentSymbol.selectionRange.start.line, span paren depth across newlines, strip balanced [...] generic brackets, split on commas at depth zero, drop name=... kwargs, and strip trailing [...] from each base.
claims.rationale: Defines the exact, bounded parser surface for extracting bases; parser must be exportable and unit-tested.
claims.excerpt: **Parser rules (all exportable + unit-tested):**

claims.claim: The probe fixture migrates from test/fixtures/pyright-probe/ to test/fixtures/python/ and becomes the adapter's primary test fixture.
claims.rationale: Reuses probe artifacts as conformance fixtures.
claims.excerpt: **`test/fixtures/pyright-probe/` migrates to `test/fixtures/python/`.**

claims.claim: TypeInfo.{extends, implements, usedByTypes} has the same meaning across TypeScript and Python adapters; only extraction mechanism differs.
claims.rationale: ADR-07 contract semantics must hold cross-language to avoid impact_of_change consumers special-casing.
claims.excerpt: **ADR-07 semantics hold across TypeScript and Python.** `TypeInfo.{extends, implements, usedByTypes}` has the same meaning in both adapters

claims.claim: basedpyright is not supported in v0.1; revisit requires evidence of concrete Pyright limitations basedpyright addresses.
claims.rationale: Behavior-shift divergence is unwarranted without evidence; Pyright has broader ecosystem familiarity.
claims.excerpt: **basedpyright support.** Pyright only for v0.1.

claims.claim: src/adapters/registry.ts must be updated so the 'python' case constructs a new PyrightAdapter() instead of throwing.
claims.rationale: Wires the new adapter into the plugin registry.
claims.excerpt: swaps the `"python"` case's throw statement for `new PyrightAdapter()`.

claims.claim: A single workspaceFolders initialize call covering the module root is sufficient; no per-package reinitialize
claims.rationale: Gopls indexes implementation relationships across all packages in the workspace from one root
claims.excerpt: A single `workspaceFolders` initialize call covering the module root is sufficient for gopls to index implementation relationships across all packages in the workspace. No per-package reinitialize needed.

claims.claim: Adapter flattens interface children to top-level Symbol records and must record a `parent_id` field on flattened method symbols pointing to the interface's SymbolId
claims.rationale: Flat layout matches struct-method shape; parent_id is load-bearing — flattening without it would drop interface-membership signal entirely
claims.excerpt: **Parent pointer preservation (load-bearing):** the flattened method Symbol records a `parent_id` field pointing to the interface's SymbolId.

claims.claim: Adapter outputs reduced symbol kinds: function, method, class, interface, other; struct and type-definition collapse to class
claims.rationale: ADR-01 specifies reduced kind set; collapse is intended
claims.excerpt: Per [ADR-01](ADR-01-symbol-id-format.md), the adapter outputs reduced kinds: `function`, `method`, `class`, `interface`, `other`. Struct and type-definition collapse to `class` under this reduction

claims.claim: Do not vendor gopls as an npm binary dependency; do not support Go workspaces in v0.1; do not implement build-tag filtering, receiver-name rewriting, or speculative implementation-endpoint fallbacks
claims.rationale: Explicit non-goals to scope the v0.1 adapter
claims.excerpt: Vendoring gopls as a binary dependency of the npm package. Follows the tsserver precedent: user-installed, PATH-resolved.

claims.claim: Embedded structs surface as field children (kind 8) of the embedder; promoted method tracking happens via getTypeInfo, not listSymbols; do not synthesize phantom method-child entries
claims.rationale: Preserves gopls's documentSymbol shape; promoted-method synthesis is out of scope
claims.excerpt: **Decision:** the adapter preserves the embedded-field documentSymbol shape for ingestion (B surfaces as a field of A). Promoted method tracking happens via `getTypeInfo` ... Attempting to synthesize phantom method-child entries is out of scope.

claims.claim: Every adapter, including GoAdapter, must implement getTypeInfo with the {extends, implements, usedByTypes} contract
claims.rationale: ADR-07 requires uniform type-info contract across all language adapters
claims.excerpt: ADR-07 requires every adapter — Go included — to implement `getTypeInfo` with the same `{ extends, implements, usedByTypes }` contract.

claims.claim: Generic type-parameter lists are preserved verbatim in signatures, names, and SymbolIds with no adapter-side reconstruction or normalization
claims.rationale: The type-parameter list is part of the canonical name
claims.excerpt: **Decision:** signatures, names, and SymbolIds carry generics verbatim. No adapter-side reconstruction or normalization.

claims.claim: GoAdapter implementation should land in 700-1000 LOC, smaller than pyright's 1247 LOC
claims.rationale: No Protocol/ABC fallback, no inventory-walk for getTypeInfo, no alias-resolution pass needed
claims.excerpt: Based on pyright precedent (`src/adapters/pyright.ts` is 1247 LOC), GoAdapter should land in 700-1000 LOC.

claims.claim: GoAdapter must implement a `workspace/configuration` handler that returns a length-matched array of objects, not a generic null-stub
claims.rationale: Gopls (unlike pyright) does not tolerate null responses to workspace/configuration; null causes 'no views' cascade
claims.excerpt: **Adapter responsibility:** implement this handler, do not delegate to a generic null-stub. For v0.1, returning empty objects (gopls defaults) is sufficient.

claims.claim: Gopls is user-installed via `go install`, not bundled as an npm dependency
claims.rationale: Follows the tsserver pattern: user-provided, not a bundled dependency
claims.excerpt: This follows the **tsserver pattern** from [ADR-03](ADR-03-language-adapter-plugin.md) and CLAUDE.md: "user-provided, not a bundled dependency."

claims.claim: Gopls v0.21.1+ is the sole Go LSP backend for v0.1; alternatives (homegrown go/types, go-langserver, govim wrappers) are rejected
claims.rationale: Gopls solves cross-package resolution, interface satisfaction, and generic inference; alternatives are unmaintained or rebuild what gopls already provides
claims.excerpt: **Gopls v0.21.1+** is the sole Go LSP backend for v0.1.

claims.claim: Gopls version pin must track the Go toolchain version closely; stale gopls vs newer Go silently breaks module loading
claims.rationale: Gopls's only-latest-Go build-support policy means a gopls compiled against an older Go cannot be used against a newer toolchain
claims.excerpt: Pin must track the Go toolchain version closely — gopls's "only latest Go" build-support policy means a gopls compiled against Go 1.23 cannot be used against a Go 1.26 toolchain.

claims.claim: Iota const block members are flattened to independent top-level Symbol records; do not reconstruct a synthetic block container
claims.rationale: Matches gopls's native shape; iota is syntactic sugar, not semantic grouping
claims.excerpt: **Decision:** flatten iota const members to independent top-level Symbol records. Do NOT reconstruct a synthetic block container.

claims.claim: LanguageAdapter methods map directly to specific gopls LSP requests with no fallback paths needed
claims.rationale: All methods probed cleanly against gopls v0.21.1
claims.excerpt: For each adapter method, a direct mapping to a gopls LSP request. All methods probed cleanly — no fallback paths needed.

claims.claim: Preserve gopls's receiver-encoded method names verbatim in SymbolId; do not strip parens or receiver prefix
claims.rationale: The encoding is deterministic, unique, and human-readable; pointer vs value receiver carries semantic information; uniqueness for same-named methods on different types in same file
claims.excerpt: **Decision:** preserve the gopls receiver-encoded name verbatim in SymbolId. Do NOT strip parens or receiver prefix.

claims.claim: The `go` binary must be on the gopls process's PATH; GoAdapter must validate this via a `go version` preflight and emit an actionable error if missing
claims.rationale: Gopls spawns `go` for module loading; without it gopls returns 'no views' for all requests
claims.excerpt: Gopls spawns `go` as a subprocess for module loading, dependency analysis, and build-related operations. Without it, gopls emits `"Error loading workspace folders (expected 1, got 0)"` at startup

claims.claim: The v0.1 Go adapter does not filter by build tag; it surfaces all symbols from all .go files; pipeline layer is responsible for build-tag-based dedup
claims.rationale: Keeps adapter contract clean (surface everything gopls sees); language-specific dedup semantics belong in the pipeline
claims.excerpt: **Layer responsibility (explicit):** pipeline layer is responsible for package-level dedup. Adapter surface is intentionally symbol-exhaustive

claims.claim: getTypeInfo calls gopls's `implementation` endpoint once and partitions results by interface-vs-concrete; no inventory-walk fallback is implemented
claims.rationale: Gopls's implementation endpoint is complete (unlike pyright's); defensive fallback would add untested code
claims.excerpt: **Decision:** `getTypeInfo` calls `implementation` once, partitions results by whether each result location is an interface-type or concrete-type declaration

claims.claim: v0.1 Go adapter supports only single-module setups; no go.work workspaces, no multi-module, no GOPATH, no vendor-mode special-casing, no cgo verification, no //go:generate semantics
claims.rationale: Documented limitations of the v0.1 adapter scope
claims.excerpt: **Single-module only.** The v0.1 Go adapter does not support Go workspaces (`go.work`) or multi-module setups.

claims.claim: Cap of 10 is an adjustable sub-decision; the locked contract is the array shape, not the integer.
claims.rationale: Allows post-Phase-8 evidence to drive cap adjustment without an ADR amendment.
claims.excerpt: **Cap is an adjustable sub-decision.** ... The contract change this ADR locks is the **shape** (single-or-array `symbol` input), not the specific numeric cap.

claims.claim: Compact-format multi-symbol responses must use the delimiter line `--- get_symbol_context: <symbol> (N of M) ---` with caller's input string verbatim.
claims.rationale: Encodes input identifier and positional context so consumers can map sub-bundles back when partial failures occur; part of wire contract.
claims.excerpt:
Per-symbol sub-bundles are concatenated, each preceded by a delimiter line:

```
--- get_symbol_context: <symbol> (N of M) ---

claims.claim: Dedup is exact-string-match with .trim() whitespace normalization, applied before resolution; case-sensitive; SymbolId vs plain name produce separate slots.
claims.rationale: Input-string-keyed dedup preserves caller mental model; resolution-keyed dedup complicates partial-failure rendering.
claims.excerpt: **Dedup is exact-string-match with `.trim()` whitespace normalization, applied before resolution.**

claims.claim: Extend the existing get_symbol_context tool to accept either a string or string array; do not add a new tool.
claims.rationale: MCP tool selection is by tool name not input shape; preserves three-primitive framing and gives free backward compatibility for single-symbol callers.
claims.excerpt: The existing `get_symbol_context` tool is extended to accept either a single string or a string array. **No new tool is added.**

claims.claim: JSON-format multi-symbol response uses a `results` array where each entry has the input string verbatim plus exactly one of `bundle` (success) or `error` (failure).
claims.rationale: Symmetric shape across success/failure simplifies consumer code walking the array.
claims.excerpt: Each entry has the caller's input string verbatim, plus exactly one of `bundle` (on success) or `error` (on per-symbol failure).

claims.claim: Maximum 10 symbols per call (MAX_SYMBOLS_PER_CALL = 10).
claims.rationale: Phase 7 c4 used 3 symbols; 10 leaves ~3× typical headroom for behavior-cluster queries while keeping bundle size predictable.
claims.excerpt: **Maximum 10 symbols per call** (`MAX_SYMBOLS_PER_CALL = 10`).

claims.claim: No atlas schema change; this is purely a query-time API surface change.
claims.rationale: Avoids extraction pipeline and storage impact.
claims.excerpt: **No atlas schema change.** This is purely a query-time API surface change.

claims.claim: No server-side warning is emitted for token budget; multi-symbol deep calls can exceed model context windows in pathological cases.
claims.rationale: Token budget is the caller's concern; per-symbol max_refs is the caller's lever.
claims.excerpt: a 10-symbol deep-depth call can still exceed a model's context window in pathological cases. No server-side warning is emitted.

claims.claim: Per-symbol failures (not_found, disambiguation_required, no_adapter) must be rendered as ERR sub-bundles in their positional slot; siblings render normally.
claims.rationale: Disambiguation and adapter-missing are per-symbol resolution failures, not whole-call failures; supports polyglot atlases and partial recovery.
claims.excerpt: **Per-symbol failures are inlined as ERR sub-bundles in their positional slot.**

claims.claim: Per-symbol options (depth, include, max_refs, file_hint, format) apply uniformly across all symbols in a batch; no per-symbol heterogeneity.
claims.rationale: Heterogeneous options would break the clean string|string[] schema extension; YAGNI given motivating evidence.
claims.excerpt: `depth`, `include`, `max_refs`, `file_hint`, and `format` apply **uniformly across all symbols in the batch.**

claims.claim: Response order must match request order; no reordering by relevance, language, or success status.
claims.rationale: Relevance ranking is find_by_intent's job; get_symbol_context is the 'I already know what I want' primitive.
claims.excerpt: **Response order matches request order.** ... **No reordering by relevance, language, or success status.**

claims.claim: Sending 11+ symbols must return a JSON-RPC InvalidParams McpError, not silent truncation.
claims.rationale: Cap violation is the caller using the API wrong; silent truncation is a contract hazard.
claims.excerpt: **Cap exceedance returns `McpError` (protocol-level).** When the caller sends 11+ items, the response is a JSON-RPC `InvalidParams` error

claims.claim: Single-string input must produce byte-identical legacy output (no delimiter line, no results envelope); detection is on input shape, not response.
claims.rationale: Preserves byte-level backward compatibility for every existing caller; verified by golden-output tests.
claims.excerpt: callers passing `["Foo"]` get the multi-symbol envelope shape with one entry; callers passing `"Foo"` get the legacy shape. This preserves byte-level backward compatibility for every existing caller.

claims.claim: Streaming partial results is out of scope; entire response returns at once.
claims.rationale: MCP doesn't stream tool responses; per-symbol resolution is fast enough that batched-response is simpler.
claims.excerpt: **Streaming partial results.** MCP doesn't stream tool responses; the entire compact body or JSON envelope returns at once.

claims.claim: Two distinct input strings that resolve to the same SymbolId must still produce two response slots (no resolution-keyed dedup).
claims.rationale: Dedup is an input-layer concern; resolution collisions are intentional.
claims.excerpt: Different input strings even if they resolve to the same underlying symbol. Dedup is an input-layer concern

claims.claim: When file_hint is provided alongside multi-symbol input, it applies to every name resolution in the batch.
claims.rationale: Supports common case of related symbols in the same directory; per-symbol hints require separate calls or full SymbolId.
claims.excerpt: When `file_hint` is provided alongside a multi-symbol input, it applies to every name resolution.

claims.claim: Whole-call isError:true is set only when EVERY symbol in the batch failed; partial failures inline ERR sub-bundles with isError:false.
claims.rationale: All-or-nothing semantics would forfeit the c4 use case and make multi-symbol a strictly weaker substitute for grep-OR.
claims.excerpt: **Whole-call `isError: true` is set only when EVERY symbol in the batch failed to resolve.**

claims.claim: impact_of_change and find_by_intent remain unchanged in v0.3; multi-symbol variants are deferred.
claims.rationale: Multi-symbol blast-radius and multi-query intent semantics are separate design problems with their own ranking/dedup concerns.
claims.excerpt: **`impact_of_change` is unchanged in v0.3.** ... **`find_by_intent` is unchanged in v0.3.**

claims.claim: parseArgs normalizes input to a string[] internal shape (single string → length-1 array), then fans out the existing per-symbol path with shared options.
claims.rationale: Implementation pattern that reuses existing single-symbol resolve→buildBundle→render path.
claims.excerpt: `parseArgs` learns to accept array input, normalizes to a `string[]` internal shape (single string → length-1 array), then fans out the existing per-symbol path

claims.claim: Handler parseArgs trims whitespace on query and treats empty string as absent
claims.rationale: Handler-level normalization for the optional query field
claims.excerpt: `parseArgs` parses optional `query`; trims whitespace; treats empty as absent.

claims.claim: ADR-09's find_by_intent BM25 chain (with name-overlap) is unchanged by this ADR
claims.rationale: No breaking change to ADR-09; symmetry adjustment is local to get_symbol_context
claims.excerpt: **No breaking change to ADR-09.** Find-by-intent's BM25 chain (with name-overlap) stays as documented.

claims.claim: BM25 ranking from ADR-09 is extended from find_by_intent to get_symbol_context's intent block, gated behind an optional caller-provided query parameter
claims.rationale: Addresses Gap 2 (query-aware ranking) + Gap 3 (tool asymmetry) identified in Phase 6 §5.1's muddy-bundle mechanism
claims.excerpt: This ADR addresses Gap 2 + Gap 3 directly by extending ADR-09's BM25 ranking primitive from `find_by_intent` to `get_symbol_context`'s intent block, gated behind an optional caller-provided query parameter.

claims.claim: BM25 ranking is stateless; no query history or user feedback loops in v0.3
claims.rationale: Future evidence-gated extension if real usage patterns motivate it
claims.excerpt: **No query history / user feedback loops.** BM25 is stateless; v0.3 doesn't track which queries hit which claims or use that to tune ranking.

claims.claim: CANARY 1: when bm25Query is absent, claim ranking must be byte-identical to v0.2 (severity → source → claim_id)
claims.rationale: Ship-blocker canary protects v0.2 backward compatibility contract
claims.excerpt: **CANARY 1** — `bm25Query` absent: claim ranking is byte-identical to v0.2 (severity → source → claim_id). Tested directly at the `buildBundle` API.

claims.claim: CANARY 2: when bm25Query is present, BM25 path activates and reorders claims
claims.rationale: Ship-blocker canary tested with Phase 6 §5.1 muddy-bundle pattern
claims.excerpt: **CANARY 2** — `bm25Query` present: BM25 path activates and reorders claims. Tested with the Phase 6 §5.1 muddy-bundle pattern

claims.claim: Cross-symbol BM25 ranking remains find_by_intent's territory; get_symbol_context BM25 scores claims attached to a single symbol only
claims.rationale: Preserves clear tool separation between get_symbol_context (everything for a symbol) and find_by_intent (relevance across symbols)
claims.excerpt: **Single-symbol claims subset only.** This BM25 path scores claims attached to a single symbol. Cross-symbol BM25 ranking ... is `find_by_intent`'s territory, not extended here.

claims.claim: Embedding-based ranking is deferred to v0.4+; v0.3 uses FTS5 BM25 exclusively
claims.rationale: Per ADR-09 + DESIGN.md 'don't embed' rule
claims.excerpt: **Embedding-based ranking.** Explicitly deferred to v0.4+ per ADR-09 + DESIGN.md "don't embed" rule. This ADR uses FTS5 BM25 exclusively.

claims.claim: Future readers MUST NOT weaken the canary assertions during refactors
claims.rationale: Canary discipline forms a 3-data-point pattern across v0.3 work (ADR-15, Step 5, ADR-16)
claims.excerpt: future readers MUST NOT weaken these assertions during refactors.

claims.claim: In multi-symbol mode, the query parameter applies uniformly to every symbol in the batch; no per-symbol query overrides
claims.rationale: Matches ADR-15 §3's uniform-per-symbol-options rule for depth, include, max_refs, and file_hint
claims.excerpt: When `get_symbol_context` is called with `symbol: string[]` (ADR-15 multi-symbol mode), the `query` parameter applies uniformly to every symbol in the batch. No per-symbol query overrides.

claims.claim: New `mcp` config section established; future MCP-server query-time knobs land in the same section
claims.rationale: Establishes location for future MCP query-time configuration
claims.excerpt: **Optional config flag location** (`mcp.symbol_context_bm25`). New `mcp` config section established here; future MCP-server query-time knobs land in the same section.

claims.claim: No atlas schema change required; this is purely query-time and claims_fts is already populated by ADR-09
claims.rationale: Constrains scope to query-time only; no re-extraction needed
claims.excerpt: **No atlas schema change.** This is purely query-time; `claims_fts` is already populated by ADR-09's existing infrastructure.

claims.claim: No diversity / clustering post-processing applied to BM25-ranked claims
claims.rationale: Top-N could be tightly clustered around one sub-topic; flagged as post-v0.1 concern
claims.excerpt: **No diversity / clustering post-processing.** When many claims match the query ... BM25 ranks them by match strength without diversification.

claims.claim: No name-overlap tiebreaker is used in get_symbol_context's BM25 chain (departure from ADR-09)
claims.rationale: All claims belong to a single symbol so they share the same name-overlap score; the tiebreaker is degenerate
claims.excerpt: **No name-overlap tiebreaker** (departure from ADR-09's chain). In `get_symbol_context`, all claims belong to a single symbol — they all share the same name-overlap score against any query.

claims.claim: Non-matching claims must still surface in the bundle (sorted to end via +Infinity sentinel), not be filtered out
claims.rationale: get_symbol_context's contract is 'give me everything,' not 'give me the relevant subset'; filtering is find_by_intent's job
claims.excerpt: **POSITIVE_INFINITY sentinel for unmatched claims.** Preserves the rule "all claims attached to the symbol surface in the bundle" — non-matching claims sort to the end rather than being filtered out.

claims.claim: Reuse ADR-09's exported BM25 primitives (sanitizeQuery, buildMatchQuery) from src/queries/find-by-intent.ts
claims.rationale: Reuse minimizes implementation surface and ensures the two tools' BM25 semantics stay aligned
claims.excerpt: `sanitizeQuery` and `buildMatchQuery` are already exported from `src/queries/find-by-intent.ts`. The BM25 SQL is a small variant of the existing find_by_intent query, filtered to a single symbol's claim subset. Reuse minimizes implementation surface

claims.claim: Server config flag `mcp.symbol_context_bm25` (boolean) gates the BM25 path; defaults to false
claims.rationale: Default-off keeps v0.2 callers byte-equivalent and gives Step 7 the on/off toggle for ship decisions
claims.excerpt: A new config field `mcp.symbol_context_bm25: boolean` controls whether the BM25 path is available at all. Defaults to `false` (v0.2 baseline preserved).

claims.claim: Server-derived query strings are explicitly out of scope (non-goal)
claims.rationale: Inference of 'what query does this symbol imply' is design-debate territory with no clear right answer
claims.excerpt: **Server-derived query strings.** Any "what query does this symbol imply?" inference logic is rejected as design-debate territory

claims.claim: ServerRuntimeContext gains optional symbolContextBM25 field plumbed from config
claims.rationale: Server context wiring required to propagate the flag
claims.excerpt: `ServerRuntimeContext.symbolContextBM25?` plumbed from config.

claims.claim: Two-layer gating: BM25 activates only when both server flag is true AND caller passes a query parameter
claims.rationale: Either alone is insufficient; both together give Step 7 the configuration matrix it needs
claims.excerpt: When `true`, BM25 activates only when the caller also passes a `query` parameter — **two-layer gating**

claims.claim: When query absent or flag off, fall back to v0.2 sort: severity → source → claim_id
claims.rationale: Preserves backward compatibility for v0.2 callers
claims.excerpt:
When `query` is absent OR flag is off, the chain falls back to v0.2:

1. **Severity** (hard > soft > context)
2. **Source alphabetical**
3. **Claim id ASC** (insertion order)

claims.claim: When query provided and flag on, sort chain is: BM25 ASC → Severity → Source alphabetical → Claim id ASC
claims.rationale: BM25 must dominate (chain α) because severity-first ordering is precisely what Phase 6 §5.1 documented as broken in muddy-bundle case
claims.excerpt:
1. **BM25 ASC** (more negative = better; unmatched = `+Infinity` → sorts last)
2. **Severity** (hard > soft > context)
3. **Source alphabetical**
4. **Claim id ASC** (final deterministic fallback)

claims.claim: bm25Query only flows from handler to buildBundle when both server flag is on AND caller query is present
claims.rationale: Implements the two-layer gating at the handler layer
claims.excerpt: Two-layer gating in `resolveSingle`: `bm25Query` only flows to `buildBundle` when both server flag is on AND caller query is present.

claims.claim: buildBundle branches between sortClaimsByBM25 and sortClaimsBySeverityThenSource based on bm25Query presence
claims.rationale: Defines the query-layer dispatch between BM25 and v0.2 deterministic ordering
claims.excerpt: New `sortClaimsByBM25` helper; `buildBundle` branches between `sortClaimsByBM25` and `sortClaimsBySeverityThenSource` based on `bm25Query` presence.

claims.claim: get_symbol_context's tool input schema gains an optional `query` field; query is caller-provided, not server-derived
claims.rationale: Caller-driven querying preserves ADR-02's no-query-time-LLM rule and matches find_by_intent precedent for symmetric tool surface
claims.excerpt: `get_symbol_context`'s tool input schema gains an optional `query` field. When provided, the server-side BM25 path activates (subject to flag gating, §3 below). When absent, ranking falls back to v0.2 deterministic order

claims.claim: Ambiguous symbols (overloaded names in the same file) currently collide — first declaration wins.
claims.rationale: Known MVP limitation; future major version may add disambiguator.
claims.excerpt: Ambiguous cases (overloaded names in the same file) currently collide — first declaration wins.

claims.claim: At index time, Opus 4.7 extracts structured claims of {symbol, constraint, severity, rationale, source} from ADRs/docs, written to SQLite keyed to LSP-resolved symbol IDs.
claims.rationale: The intent registry is the novel architectural layer.
claims.excerpt: Opus 4.7 reads every ADR, design doc, and structured docstring in the repo, and extracts structured claims of the form {symbol, constraint, severity, rationale, source}.

claims.claim: Atlas is fully loadable with no streaming or chunking for MVP.
claims.rationale: MVP scope decision.
claims.excerpt: Fully loadable. No streaming, no chunking for MVP.

claims.claim: Atlas schema versioning is additive within minor versions; breaking schema changes require major version bumps and automatic migration.
claims.rationale: Versioning policy for schema evolution.
claims.excerpt: Versioned schema. ... Breaking schema changes require major version bumps and automatic migration.

claims.claim: BM25 only re-orders intent claims; it never filters. Non-matching claims sort to the end via a +Infinity sentinel.
claims.rationale: Preserves the 'give me everything attached to this symbol' contract.
claims.excerpt: Claims that don't match any query token still surface in the bundle but sort to the end via a +Infinity sentinel ... BM25 only re-orders, never filters.

claims.claim: BM25 ranking on get_symbol_context activates only with two-layer gating: server flag mcp.symbol_context_bm25 enabled AND caller passes query parameter; otherwise falls back to v0.2 deterministic ordering.
claims.rationale: Preserves byte-equivalence for existing callers.
claims.excerpt: Two-layer gating: flag-off OR query-absent both fall back to v0.2 deterministic ordering ... preserving byte-equivalence for existing callers.

claims.claim: BM25 sort chain is BM25 → severity (hard > soft > context) → source → claim_id.
claims.rationale: Defines deterministic ordering when BM25 is active.
claims.excerpt: The sort chain is BM25 → severity (hard > soft > context) → source → claim_id.

claims.claim: Bundles return compact summaries with stable IDs; Claude pulls detail by ID only when needed.
claims.rationale: Progressive disclosure avoids context pollution.
claims.excerpt: Progressive disclosure over firehose. Bundles return compact summaries with stable IDs.

claims.claim: Compact output format is the default for tool responses; JSON is available via format: 'json' parameter for programmatic consumers.
claims.rationale: Compact format produces ~40-60% token savings vs JSON with no measurable loss in Claude's ability to use the information.
claims.excerpt: Empirically produces ~40-60% token savings vs JSON on the same content ... JSON is available for programmatic consumers.

claims.claim: Day-4 scope gate: if the get_symbol_context primitive is not rock-solid by end of day 4, do not proceed to the composites.
claims.rationale: Protect the primitive at all costs; enforced by CLAUDE.md.
claims.excerpt: if the primitive is not rock-solid by end of day 4, do not proceed to the composites. Protect the primitive at all costs.

claims.claim: Duplicate input symbols are dropped via .trim()-normalized exact-string-match dedup before resolution.
claims.rationale: Defines dedup semantics for batch input.
claims.excerpt: Duplicate input strings are dropped via .trim()-normalized exact-string-match dedup before resolution.

claims.claim: Every tool call should maximize density: a single get_symbol_context call must replace 8-15 grep/read/blame round-trips.
claims.rationale: Fixed overhead per tool call; the win is density, not speed.
claims.excerpt: Do more per tool call. Every tool call has fixed overhead. The win is density, not speed.

claims.claim: Extraction prompt is versioned and the model string is locked in config to mitigate prompt drift across model updates.
claims.rationale: Future model versions may produce different output shapes.
claims.excerpt: Mitigation: version the prompt, log extraction output for regression review, lock model string in config.

claims.claim: Extraction uses Opus 4.7 at default effort, not extended thinking; extended thinking is an escape hatch, not the default.
claims.rationale: Keeps extraction cost at ~$0.25 per substantial ADR.
claims.excerpt: We use Opus 4.7 at default effort, not extended thinking. ... Extended thinking is available as an escape hatch ... but is not the default.

claims.claim: Go adapter requires a PATH-resolved go binary and length-matched workspace/configuration response; receiver-encoded struct method names are preserved verbatim; interface methods are flattened with parent_id back-pointer.
claims.rationale: gopls-specific runtime prerequisites and structural decisions per ADR-14.
claims.excerpt: ADR-14 documents the gopls-specific runtime prerequisites (PATH-resolved go binary, length-matched workspace/configuration response) and structural decisions

claims.claim: High-effort reasoning (Opus extraction) happens once per source change; queries are dictionary lookups.
claims.rationale: Index-time work, query-time lookups principle.
claims.excerpt: Index-time work, query-time lookups. High-effort reasoning ... happens once per source change. Queries are dictionary lookups.

claims.claim: In symbol resolution, exact matches link; ambiguous matches keep all candidates; non-matches are dropped and logged as potential hallucinations.
claims.rationale: Defines stage-4 resolution semantics.
claims.excerpt: Exact matches are linked; ambiguous matches keep all candidates; non-matches are dropped (and logged as potential hallucinations).

claims.claim: Incremental reindex hashes every source file and only re-processes changed files; stale claims for changed files are deleted before re-running the pipeline.
claims.rationale: SHA-based invalidation ensures no wasted work.
claims.excerpt: Hash every source file. On reindex, compare current file SHAs ... Only re-process changed files. Delete stale claims for changed files

claims.claim: MVP excludes additional language adapters beyond TS/Python/Go, cross-repo symbol resolution, monorepo workspaces, embedding-based search, query logging/hot-path pre-computation, web UI, and VS Code extension.
claims.rationale: Explicit scope-gate boundaries.
claims.excerpt: Out of scope for MVP: Additional language adapters ... Cross-repo symbol resolution ... Embedding-based semantic search

claims.claim: Multi-symbol call returns isError: true only when every symbol fails to resolve; per-symbol failures inline as ERR sub-bundles in their positional slot.
claims.rationale: Defines partial-failure semantics.
claims.excerpt: Per-symbol failures inline as ERR sub-bundles in their positional slot; the call returns isError: true only when *every* symbol failed to resolve.

claims.claim: Multi-symbol mode accepts up to 10 symbols; cap exceedance (11+ items) raises MCP InvalidParams rather than silently truncating.
claims.rationale: Explicit error is the cleaner failure mode.
claims.excerpt: Cap exceedance (11+ items) raises an MCP InvalidParams protocol error rather than silently truncating — explicit error is the cleaner failure mode.

claims.claim: Multi-symbol mode applies the same query uniformly to every symbol in the batch (uniform-options rule).
claims.rationale: Per ADR-15 §3.
claims.excerpt: Multi-symbol mode applies the same query uniformly to every symbol in the batch (per ADR-15 §3 uniform-options rule).

claims.claim: Optional atlas fields use omit-when-empty: signature, parent_id, rationale, excerpt are omitted when empty/null/undefined; no field may be added that distinguishes between absent, null, and empty string.
claims.rationale: Round-trip invariant collapses those states.
claims.excerpt: no field may be added that requires preserving a distinction between absent, null, and empty string — round-trip collapses those states.

claims.claim: Per-repo config .contextatlas.yml requires seven sections (extraction is optional); no inheritance, no workspaces, no cross-repo refs.
claims.rationale: MVP-scoped deliberately.
claims.excerpt: Seven sections required (extraction is optional). No inheritance, no workspaces, no cross-repo refs. MVP-scoped deliberately.

claims.claim: Push expensive understanding to index time; serve queries cheaply from a durable index keyed to LSP-resolved symbols.
claims.rationale: Core architectural move addressing token burn, invisible intent, and stateless sessions.
claims.excerpt: push expensive understanding to index time, serve queries cheaply from a durable index keyed to LSP-resolved symbols.

claims.claim: Query-time cost is zero LLM calls — pure SQLite lookups plus LSP queries; sub-100ms per get_symbol_context call on typical hardware.
claims.rationale: Performance contract from index-time/query-time split.
claims.excerpt: Query-time cost: Zero LLM calls. Pure SQLite lookups + LSP queries. Sub-100ms per get_symbol_context call

claims.claim: Reference IDs follow ref:<lang-short-code>:<path>:<line>; unlike Symbol IDs, Reference IDs include line because a reference is a location.
claims.rationale: Distinguishes reference identity from symbol identity.
claims.excerpt: Reference IDs follow the pattern ref:<lang-short-code>:<path>:<line>. Unlike Symbol IDs, Reference IDs include line

claims.claim: Severity taxonomy: hard = explicit constraint (must/never); soft = preference (should/prefer); context = background information.
claims.rationale: Canonical severity definitions for extraction.
claims.excerpt: hard — explicit constraint, violation is a bug ... soft — preference, violation is a smell ... context — background information, no rule

claims.claim: Signal sources (LSP, intent, git, tests) are independent; fusion happens at query time, not ingest time.
claims.rationale: Keeps indexing cheap and lets each query request only the signals it needs.
claims.excerpt: Signal sources are independent. ... Fusion happens at query time, not ingest time.

claims.claim: Single-string input preserves legacy single-bundle output shape; ['Foo'] and 'Foo' produce different shapes by design.
claims.rationale: Backward compatibility for legacy callers.
claims.excerpt: Single-string input preserves the legacy single-bundle output shape ... ['Foo'] and 'Foo' produce different shapes by design.

claims.claim: Stage 0 atlas import: when a committed atlas.json exists, import symbols/claims into local SQLite and use committed SHAs as the baseline before running stages 1-5.
claims.rationale: Avoids paying full first-run cost for new contributors.
claims.excerpt: Before doing any extraction, check for a committed atlas.json. If present: 1. Import all symbols and claims into local SQLite 2. Record the committed SHAs as the baseline

claims.claim: Symbol names, references, types, and diagnostics must always come from the language server, never from regex.
claims.rationale: LSP is the ground truth; compiler-grade precision is required.
claims.excerpt: Symbol names, references, types, diagnostics — always from the language server, never from regex. Compiler-grade precision or nothing.

claims.claim: Under chain α BM25, context-severity claims may be promoted above hard-severity claims; callers should treat top-INTENT under BM25 as 'most-relevant-to-this-query' not 'most-severe-constraint-overall'.
claims.rationale: Documented caveat from spot-check evidence.
claims.excerpt: the chain α design (BM25 dominates; severity is tiebreaker, not primary sort) may promote context-severity claims above hard-severity claims

claims.claim: atlas.committed: false is a supported degraded mode; the system must work correctly under both committed and non-committed settings.
claims.rationale: Regulated/internal teams may not be able to commit atlas.
claims.excerpt: This is the degraded mode, but it's a supported degraded mode. The system must work correctly under both settings.

claims.claim: atlas.json must use deterministic ordering: symbols sorted by ID, claims sorted by (source, symbol_id, claim).
claims.rationale: Keeps git diffs focused and readable.
claims.excerpt: Deterministic ordering. Symbols sorted by ID. Claims sorted by (source, symbol_id, claim).

claims.claim: atlas.json ↔ index.db sync must be lossless and idempotent; round-tripping atlas.json through SQLite produces a byte-identical file.
claims.rationale: Loss of fidelity would cause committed atlas to drift from reality across team members.
claims.excerpt: Lossless — round-tripping atlas.json through SQLite back to atlas.json produces a byte-identical file. ... Both directions must preserve all data.

claims.claim: drop-with-fallback recovers zero-symbol claim attribution but does not recover partial-loss cases.
claims.rationale: Documented narrowing risk for default-on behavior.
claims.excerpt: drop-with-fallback recovers zero-symbol cases but does NOT recover partial-loss cases.

claims.claim: extraction.budget_warn_usd is not a hard cap — when exceeded a single warning is logged to stderr and the run continues.
claims.rationale: Defines budget-warn semantics.
claims.excerpt: Not a hard cap — the run continues.

claims.claim: find_by_intent uses SQL text matching (LIKE or FTS5) against the claims table; no embeddings or vector search for MVP.
claims.rationale: Structured claim text matches well without vector pipeline complexity; embeddings deferred pending benchmark evidence.
claims.excerpt: No embeddings, no vector search for MVP — the extracted claim text is already structured prose

claims.claim: get_symbol_context is the primitive; find_by_intent and impact_of_change are thin composites reusing the same index, extraction output, and symbol resolution.
claims.rationale: Architectural reuse: composites must not introduce new subsystems.
claims.excerpt: find_by_intent and impact_of_change are thin composites over the primitive — reusing the same index, the same extraction output, and the same symbol resolution layer.

claims.claim: impact_of_change is implemented by composition: calls get_symbol_context internally and adds git co-change and test-impact analysis.
claims.rationale: Composite implementation strategy.
claims.excerpt: Implementation is composition: calls get_symbol_context internally, adds git co-change analysis ... and test-impact analysis

claims.claim: v0.3 ships extraction.narrow_attribution as drop-with-fallback default-on; opt-out is explicit narrow_attribution: off.
claims.rationale: Step 7 ship default; Pattern 2 commits flag-off codepath maintenance through v0.3-v0.5+.
claims.excerpt: v0.3 ships drop-with-fallback as default-on per STEP-PLAN-V0.3 Step 7 progress log.

claims.claim: Claim-to-symbol relationship is many-to-many; a single claim frequently references multiple symbols.
claims.rationale: Empirically confirmed during extraction testing.
claims.excerpt: Many-to-many between claims and symbols — a single claim frequently references multiple symbols

claims.claim: source_shas tracks prose documents fed to extraction; file_sha on symbols tracks code files; they have distinct invalidation triggers and storage.
claims.rationale: Distinct concerns for prose-doc invalidation vs source-file invalidation.
claims.excerpt: source_shas is deliberately a separate concept from the file_sha column on symbols ... Distinct invalidation triggers, distinct storage.

claims.claim: Language short codes come from the authoritative LANG_CODES constant in src/types.ts; changing an existing short code is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.
claims.rationale: Stable identifier contract.
claims.excerpt: Changing an existing short code is a breaking change requiring a major version bump.

claims.claim: Language adapters are plugins implementing a consistent interface (listSymbols, getSymbolDetails, findReferences, getDiagnostics) so new languages can be added without modifying core.
claims.rationale: Stable adapter interface enables contributor-friendly language additions.
claims.excerpt: Adapters are plugins. Each implements a consistent interface so new languages can be added without modifying core.

claims.claim: Symbol ID format is sym:<lang-short-code>:<path>:<n>; line numbers must not be part of the ID and live as a field on the Symbol record.
claims.rationale: Keeps IDs stable across line moves and atlas.json diffs reviewable.
claims.excerpt: Line numbers are not part of the ID. They live as a field on the Symbol record (line). This keeps IDs stable across line moves

claims.claim: Path normalization is required at every ingest boundary; all paths must use forward-slash separators regardless of OS, applied via a single normalizePath() utility.
claims.rationale: Without this, the same file on different OSes produces different IDs, breaking team consistency.
claims.excerpt: Path normalization is required at every ingest boundary. All paths ... use forward-slash separators regardless of OS. A single normalizePath() utility must be applied

claims.claim: All structural questions are delegated to the language server (tsserver, Pyright, gopls); ContextAtlas does not derive structure heuristically.
claims.rationale: Architectural bet: LSP-grounded vs heuristic-extracted.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas delegates all structural questions to the language server (tsserver, Pyright).

claims.claim: All three language adapters share an identical behavioral contract enforced by an adapter conformance test suite.
claims.rationale: Cross-language consistency.
claims.excerpt: Adapter conformance test suite (identical behavioral contract across all three adapters)

claims.claim: Architectural claims extracted from ADRs must be keyed to the specific code symbols they govern.
claims.rationale: Ensures Claude sees constraints attached to the symbol being modified.
claims.excerpt: structured claims extracted by Opus 4.7 at index time, keyed to specific code symbols

claims.claim: Architecture is designed so any subset of signals (LSP, git, tests, ADRs) produces value even without ADRs present.
claims.rationale: Graceful degradation when some signals are missing.
claims.excerpt: The architecture is designed so any subset of signals produces value.

claims.claim: Architecture is five layers: MCP interface, query fusion, signal sources, extraction pipeline, storage.
claims.rationale: Layered design with single-responsibility per layer.
claims.excerpt: Five layers, each with one job

claims.claim: At index time, text contents of ADRs, READMEs, and configured markdown docs are sent to Anthropic's API.
claims.rationale: Privacy/data flow disclosure.
claims.excerpt: Text contents of ADRs, READMEs, and other markdown docs configured via `.contextatlas.yml`

claims.claim: At query time, all resolution is local (SQLite + LSP); no network traffic or model calls.
claims.rationale: Runtime privacy and performance guarantee.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas performs a local SQLite lookup plus local LSP calls. No network traffic. No model calls.

claims.claim: Atlas schema v1.2 adds `parent_id` support for flattened-child symbols, required for Go's interface method handling (ADR-14).
claims.rationale: Schema requirement driven by Go adapter.
claims.excerpt: atlas schema v1.2 — adds `parent_id` support for flattened-child symbols, required by ADR-14's interface method handling

claims.claim: Benchmark harness lives in a separate repository so benchmarks measure the published package rather than an internal monorepo build.
claims.rationale: Integrity of benchmark methodology.
claims.excerpt: Keeping the harness out of this repo means the benchmarks measure the published `contextatlas` package's actual behavior rather than an internal monorepo build.

claims.claim: Claims have severity labels (e.g. hard/soft) attached to architectural intent, e.g. 'must be idempotent' as hard.
claims.rationale: Severity is a first-class part of claims.
claims.excerpt: INTENT ADR-07 hard "must be idempotent"

claims.claim: ContextAtlas fuses four signals (LSP structural data, architectural intent from ADRs, git history, test associations) into single-call MCP context bundles.
claims.rationale: Core architectural premise; bundles combine independent signals about a symbol.
claims.excerpt: Every bundle Claude receives combines four independent signals about a symbol

claims.claim: ContextAtlas produces a committable team artifact `atlas.json` that lives in the repo alongside code and ADRs.
claims.rationale: Turns the tool into a team asset with version-controlled architectural state.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas produces a **committable team artifact** — `atlas.json` — that lives in the repo alongside your code and ADRs.

claims.claim: Currently only markdown ADRs with YAML frontmatter are supported as intent sources.
claims.rationale: Current limitation; other formats are welcome extensions.
claims.excerpt: Currently we support markdown ADRs with YAML frontmatter.

claims.claim: Default output format is compact; JSON format is opt-in.
claims.rationale: Output format defaults.
claims.excerpt: Compact output format (default) + JSON format (opt-in)

claims.claim: Extracted claims database is stored locally at `.contextatlas/index.db` by default.
claims.rationale: Default local storage path.
claims.excerpt: The extracted claims database (`.contextatlas/index.db` by default)

claims.claim: MCP tools return pre-composed fused bundles in one call rather than exposing graph primitives for callers to compose.
claims.rationale: Architectural choice differentiating from graph-first tools.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas's MCP tools return fused bundles in one call.

claims.claim: MVP language support is TypeScript and Python; Go added in v0.2.
claims.rationale: Current language support scope.
claims.excerpt: **MVP:** TypeScript and Python.

claims.claim: On first run, if a committed `atlas.json` exists, ContextAtlas imports it directly without making extraction API calls.
claims.rationale: Avoids redundant extraction cost and onboarding delay.
claims.excerpt: On first run, ContextAtlas imports the committed atlas directly into their local cache — no extraction API calls

claims.claim: Only files whose SHAs have changed since the last index get reprocessed on subsequent runs (incremental reindex).
claims.rationale: Performance design for repeated indexing.
claims.excerpt: only files whose SHAs have changed since the last index get reprocessed

claims.claim: Python projects require Pyright on the PATH.
claims.rationale: Required language server for Python adapter.
claims.excerpt: Python projects additionally require Pyright on the PATH

claims.claim: Requires Node.js 20 or newer.
claims.rationale: Stated runtime requirement.
claims.excerpt: Node.js 20 or newer.

claims.claim: Setting `atlas.committed: false` disables the team artifact pattern; each developer runs their own extraction.
claims.rationale: Configurable fallback for teams that cannot commit the atlas.
claims.excerpt: Set `atlas.committed: false` in the config. Every developer runs their own extraction.

claims.claim: Source code, git history, LSP symbol data, and runtime query contents are never sent off the machine.
claims.rationale: Privacy guarantee.
claims.excerpt:
**Never sent anywhere:**
- Your source code
- Your git history
- LSP symbol data (names, references, types)
- Query contents at runtime

claims.claim: Storage is SQLite, SHA-keyed for incremental reindex.
claims.rationale: Storage design.
claims.excerpt: **Storage.** SQLite index, SHA-keyed for incremental reindex.

claims.claim: Storage uses claim-first model: discrete claims with severity labels keyed to symbols, not a node/edge graph.
claims.rationale: Optimized for 'what constrains this symbol?' queries.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas stores discrete claims with severity labels, optimized for "what constrains this symbol?"

claims.claim: Symbol-keyed claims chosen over embedding-based search because LSP symbols are exact while embeddings are fuzzy.
claims.rationale: Exactness preferred for code.
claims.excerpt: Embeddings are fuzzy; LSP symbols are exact. For code, exactness wins.

claims.claim: Target codebase shape is 20–300 files (average developer projects), not large monorepos.
claims.rationale: Defines the design scope and target user.
claims.excerpt: Most developers work on projects in the 20 to 300 file range... That's the codebase shape ContextAtlas targets.

claims.claim: `find_by_intent` uses FTS5 + BM25 ranking over the claims table (per ADR-09).
claims.rationale: Implementation choice for intent search.
claims.excerpt: `find_by_intent` — thin composite, FTS5 + BM25 ranking over the claims table (ADR-09)

claims.claim: `impact_of_change` composes the primitive with git co-change and test-impact data.
claims.rationale: Composite tool composition.
claims.excerpt: `impact_of_change` — thin composite, primitive + git co-change + test-impact data

claims.claim: `typescript-language-server` is declared as a peer dependency rather than a direct dependency so users control the version.
claims.rationale: User controls language server version.
claims.excerpt: ContextAtlas declares `typescript-language-server` as a **peer dependency** rather than a direct one, so you control the version.

claims.claim: The language adapter interface is a stable plugin surface; new languages are additive contributions, not core changes.
claims.rationale: Stability guarantee for extension point.
claims.excerpt: The language adapter interface is a stable plugin surface — each new language is an additive contribution, not a core change.

claims.claim: All 24 prompts per repo must be reported; cherry-picking flattering results is prohibited.
claims.rationale: Prevents selective reporting bias.
claims.excerpt: No cherry-picking. All 24 prompts per repo get reported. We do not report only the flattering ones.

claims.claim: All artifacts (ADRs, prompts, configs, raw outputs, grader notes) must be committed to the repository so the benchmark is auditable end-to-end.
claims.rationale: Auditability is a core goal of the methodology.
claims.excerpt: All artifacts — ADRs, prompts, configs, raw outputs, grader notes — are committed to the repository. The benchmark is auditable end-to-end.

claims.claim: Baseline Claude Code must receive all tools it normally has (including native LSP if configured).
claims.rationale: Comparison must be against Claude Code as shipped, not a hobbled baseline.
claims.excerpt: Baseline Claude Code gets everything it normally has — including native LSP if configured.

claims.claim: Benchmark methodology must be pre-registered: rubric fixed before runs, blind grading, and all results reported.
claims.rationale: Credibility of the benchmark depends on replicability and auditability.
claims.excerpt: It is pre-registered: the rubric below is fixed before any benchmark run, grading is blind to which condition produced each output, and all results are reported — not just the favorable ones.

claims.claim: Benchmark runs must use the same commit SHA for target repos, recorded per run for reproducibility.
claims.rationale: Reproducibility requires pinning repo state.
claims.excerpt: Same commit SHA. Recorded per run so benchmarks are reproducible.

claims.claim: Benchmark targets are three repos: honojs/hono, encode/httpx, and contextatlas itself (dogfooding).
claims.rationale: Chosen for variety in size, language, and domain reflecting realistic use cases.
claims.excerpt: Three repositories, chosen to reflect different realistic use cases

claims.claim: Both conditions must use the same Claude model version (Opus 4.7), default settings, and same temperature.
claims.rationale: Fairness — prevents model differences from confounding results.
claims.excerpt: Same Claude model version on both sides. Opus 4.7, default settings, same temperature.

claims.claim: Calibration score is independent of task success and uses a 0/1/2 rubric.
claims.rationale: Measures whether expressed confidence matches correctness separately from correctness itself.
claims.excerpt: Independent of task success. Measures whether Claude's expressed confidence matches actual correctness.

claims.claim: Changes to prompts, ADRs, rubric, reporting template, or fairness rules after the first run invalidate prior results and require re-running the full set.
claims.rationale: Pre-registration integrity — changes require re-running.
claims.excerpt: Changes after the first run invalidate prior results and require re-running the full set under the new rules.

claims.claim: Correctness and Calibration scores must be assigned blind by a grader who does not know which condition produced the output.
claims.rationale: Avoids grader bias toward the new tool.
claims.excerpt: Collected by grader following the rubric below without knowing which condition produced each output.

claims.claim: Efficiency axis metrics (tool calls, input/output tokens, wall-clock time) must be collected via tool-call instrumentation without manual grading.
claims.rationale: Keeps efficiency metrics objective and automated.
claims.excerpt: Collected via tool-call instrumentation. No manual grading required.

claims.claim: Every benchmark prompt is scored on three independent axes: Efficiency, Correctness, Confidence.
claims.rationale: Separates automatic measurements from manual blind-graded assessments.
claims.excerpt: Every benchmark prompt is scored on three independent axes

claims.claim: No p-values may be reported on n=3; report medians and ranges instead of significance tests.
claims.rationale: Sample size is too small for valid significance testing.
claims.excerpt: No p-values on n=3. The sample size is too small for significance testing.

claims.claim: Numeric results must be rounded to 5% intervals or ranges — avoid false precision like '47.3% reduction'.
claims.rationale: Precision reported should match precision actually available.
claims.excerpt: No fake precision. "47.3% reduction" implies a precision we don't have. Round to 5% intervals or ranges

claims.claim: Outputs must be labeled A/B randomly and run order shuffled to eliminate primacy bias.
claims.rationale: Eliminates ordering and labeling bias in grading.
claims.excerpt: Outputs labeled A/B randomly. Grader scores without knowing which was baseline. Run order shuffled to eliminate primacy bias.

claims.claim: Prompt files are maintained at benchmarks/prompts/<repo>.md and are the exact strings used during runs.
claims.rationale: Locates canonical prompt artifacts.
claims.excerpt: are maintained in `benchmarks/prompts/<repo>.md`. These are the exact strings given to Claude during benchmark runs

claims.claim: Prompt templates must be answerable by a well-informed human developer using only the repo and its docs; insider-knowledge prompts are invalid and must be replaced.
claims.rationale: Ensures prompts measure tool capability, not hidden knowledge.
claims.excerpt: If a prompt is answerable only with insider knowledge not present anywhere in the repo or its docs, it's a bad prompt and gets replaced.

claims.claim: Prompts must be identical in wording between conditions — no prompt-engineering advantage to ContextAtlas.
claims.rationale: Ensures fair comparison between baseline and ContextAtlas.
claims.excerpt: Identical wording between conditions. No prompt-engineering advantage to ContextAtlas.

claims.claim: Prompts where ContextAtlas performs worse than baseline must still be reported.
claims.rationale: Negative results must be disclosed for credibility.
claims.excerpt: Report failures. If a prompt shows ContextAtlas performing worse than baseline, it goes in the table. No exceptions.

claims.claim: Task success is scored as a single integer 0, 1, or 2 per prompt per the defined rubric.
claims.rationale: Standardizes correctness assessment.
claims.excerpt: Per prompt, assigned a single integer.

claims.claim: Task taxonomy consists of 24 prompts per repo, split evenly as 4 prompts across 6 buckets.
claims.rationale: Ensures coverage balance across task types.
claims.excerpt: 24 prompts per repo, 4 per bucket across 6 buckets.

claims.claim: The benchmark does not claim statistical significance, generalizability beyond tested repos, architectural superiority over alternatives, or prompt-set exhaustiveness.
claims.rationale: Explicit scope limitations of the benchmark.
claims.excerpt: What We Explicitly Do Not Claim

claims.claim: Three runs per condition per prompt are required, and medians are reported for numeric metrics.
claims.rationale: Standardizes sample size and aggregation method.
claims.excerpt: Three runs per condition per prompt. Medians reported for numeric metrics.

claims.claim: Violations of ADRs marked severity: hard count as hard constraint violations; severity: soft ADR violations count separately.
claims.rationale: Ties benchmark scoring to ADR severity taxonomy.
claims.excerpt: Count of times Claude's output would violate an ADR marked `severity: hard`.

claims.claim: ADR-14 must pin gopls v0.21.1 as the probe-tested version backing Go 1.26.2
claims.rationale: gopls's 'only latest Go' build support policy means pins must track the Go toolchain closely; older versions are incompatible with current Go.
claims.excerpt: ADR-14 should document v0.21.1 as the probe-tested version and call out the version-compatibility gotcha.

claims.claim: ADR-03 amendment is not required; the language-adapter plugin interface fits gopls as-is
claims.rationale: Go-specific mapping choices are additive (like ADR-13 for Python), not revisionary to the plugin interface.
claims.excerpt: **ADR-03 amendment: NOT required.** The language-adapter plugin interface fits gopls as-is.

claims.claim: ADR-14 specifies direct use of textDocument/implementation without an inventory-walk fallback
claims.rationale: Unlike pyright which required a fallback to find Protocol/ABC implementers, gopls's implementation endpoint works directly and handles both same-package and cross-package cases symmetrically.
claims.excerpt: Gopls's implementation endpoint works directly and handles both same-package and cross-package cases. ADR-14 can specify direct use without fallback.

claims.claim: For build-tagged files, the v0.1 Go adapter preserves all per-file symbol information (option a) rather than dedup at the package level
claims.rationale: documentSymbol is per-file and surfaces symbols regardless of active build tags; preserving all symbols is simpler and defers build-target awareness to downstream consumers.
claims.excerpt: The probe recommends (a) for v0.1 Go adapter: preserve all per-file symbol information. Build-target awareness is a downstream concern beyond the adapter's scope.

claims.claim: Generic type parameters from gopls signatures and names are used as-is in Symbol records without adapter-side reconstruction
claims.rationale: Gopls preserves type parameter lists in name and detail; signatures are ready for direct inclusion in ADR-01 Symbol.signature field.
claims.excerpt: No adapter-side reconstruction needed. Signatures are ready for direct inclusion in the Symbol record's `signature` field per ADR-01.

claims.claim: Go struct methods are surfaced as top-level symbols with receiver-encoded names (e.g. `(*Rectangle).Area`), not nested as children of the struct
claims.rationale: This is fundamentally different from tsserver and pyright (which nest class methods); the receiver-encoded name is unique, deterministic, and ADR-01-conformant.
claims.excerpt: Gopls emits struct methods with names like: `(*Rectangle).Area` (pointer receiver)... NOT nested as children of the struct. This is fundamentally different from tsserver... and pyright

claims.claim: Iota const block members should be flattened to independent top-level constants rather than nested under a block container
claims.rationale: Matches gopls's native shape, simpler than reconstructing block grouping; iota is syntactic sugar rather than semantic grouping.
claims.excerpt: The probe favors flattening — simpler, matches gopls's native shape, and iota is syntactic sugar rather than semantic grouping.

claims.claim: The `go` binary must be on the gopls process's PATH at spawn time
claims.rationale: Gopls spawns `go` as a subprocess for module loading, dependency analysis, and build operations; without it, workspace folders fail to load and all LSP requests return 'no views'.
claims.excerpt: `go` binary must be on the gopls process's PATH. Evidence: during probe iteration, spawning gopls in a shell lacking `go` produced "Error loading workspace folders (expected 1, got 0)" + every subsequent LSP request returned "no views".

claims.claim: The adapter's workspace/configuration handler must return an array of the same length as the request's items[], not null
claims.rationale: Unlike pyright, gopls does not tolerate null as the response; returning null causes gopls to skip workspace view creation and produce a 'no views' cascade.
claims.excerpt: pyright tolerates `null` as the response to `workspace/configuration`; gopls does not — returning `null` causes gopls to skip workspace view creation... The LSP-spec-correct response is an array of the same length as the request's `items[]`.

claims.claim: The symbol-ingestion layer must handle the asymmetry where interface methods appear as children but struct methods appear as siblings
claims.rationale: documentSymbol returns interface methods nested in children[] while struct methods are flat top-level entries; pipeline must normalize both shapes.
claims.excerpt: the symbol-ingestion layer must handle both shapes. The cleanest normalization is to flatten the interface method children to top-level... or to walk both as declared. Either works; the adapter should pick one and document it.

claims.claim: The workspace/configuration handler must be implemented in the adapter and not delegated to a generic null-stub
claims.rationale: Generic null-stub handlers cause gopls failures; explicit length-matched array response is required.
claims.excerpt: Adapter responsibility: implement the handler; do not delegate to a generic null-stub.

claims.claim: Type alias and type definition both use LSP SymbolKind 5 (Class); disambiguate via the `detail` field or hover output
claims.rationale: Without disambiguation, aliases and distinct type definitions appear identical in symbol kind; detail string differs (e.g. `int64` vs `UserID`), and hover includes `=` for aliases.
claims.excerpt: Type alias vs type definition both use kind 5; disambiguate via the `detail` field (`"int64"` for `UserID`, `"UserID"` for `NodeID`) or hover output (alias form includes `=` in the declaration text).

claims.claim: Required LSP methods (documentSymbol, hover, definition, references, typeDefinition, implementation, publishDiagnostics) are all functional and used directly by adapter operations
claims.rationale: All core methods returned correctly-shaped data; no method returned 'method not supported' on the core adapter path.
claims.excerpt: No method returned "method not supported" or unexpectedly-shaped data on the core adapter path.

claims.claim: textDocument/definition grounds findReferences per ADR-13 precedent
claims.rationale: Definition jumping from a reference site to declaration is the invariant that anchors findReferences behavior.
claims.excerpt: Per ADR-13, definition grounds findReferences. For Go, expected behavior: definition on a *reference site* jumps to the declaration.

claims.claim: Go SymbolIds use the gopls-native receiver-encoded method name as a stable identity with `sym:go:` prefix; no adapter-side renaming
claims.rationale: Receiver-encoded names from gopls are unique and deterministic, satisfying ADR-01 SymbolId requirements directly.
claims.excerpt: `sym:go:kinds.go:(*Rectangle).Area` is a valid ADR-01-conformant SymbolId. No adapter-side renaming needed. The `sym:go:` prefix is the stable identity.

claims.claim: Completion trigger characters are '.', '[', '"', "'"; signature help triggers are '(', ',', ')'.
claims.rationale: Defines client-side trigger semantics for editor integration.
claims.excerpt: "triggerCharacters": [ ".", "[", "\"", "'" ]

claims.claim: Diagnostics include severity, an error code (e.g., reportArgumentType, reportCallIssue), source 'Pyright', and a codeDescription href to Pyright docs.
claims.rationale: Documents diagnostic shape consumers can rely on.
claims.excerpt: "code": "reportArgumentType", "source": "Pyright", "codeDescription": { "href": "https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/blob/main/docs/configuration.md#reportArgumentType" }

claims.claim: Hover on an overloaded function returns only one signature (the first @overload), not all alternates.
claims.rationale: Implies overload enumeration requires another mechanism beyond hover.
claims.excerpt:
### overloaded parse (first @overload)
...
"value": "```python\n(function) def parse(value: int) -> int\n```"

claims.claim: Pyright advertises capabilities including definitionProvider, declarationProvider, typeDefinitionProvider, referencesProvider, documentSymbolProvider, workspaceSymbolProvider, hoverProvider, renameProvider, completionProvider, signatureHelpProvider, codeActionProvider, and callHierarchyProvider.
claims.rationale: Documents the LSP capability surface Pyright exposes, used to ground ADR-13 mappings.
claims.excerpt: "definitionProvider": { "workDoneProgress": true }, "declarationProvider": { "workDoneProgress": true }, "typeDefinitionProvider": { "workDoneProgress": true }, "referencesProvider": { "workDoneProgress": true }

claims.claim: Pyright documentSymbol uses kind 5 for classes, 6 for methods, 12 for functions, 13 for variables/parameters/fields, and 14 for constants, and emits nested children for class members and inner classes.
claims.rationale: Documents observed kind codes and hierarchical structure for symbol parsing.
claims.excerpt: { "name": "Outer", "kind": 5, ... "children": [ { "name": "Inner", "kind": 5, ...

claims.claim: Pyright does not advertise an implementationProvider capability.
claims.rationale: Absence from initialize result indicates implementation requests are not supported.
claims.excerpt: "callHierarchyProvider": true, "workspace": { "workspaceFolders": ...

claims.claim: Pyright hover represents type aliases (bare assignment, TypeAlias annotation, and PEP 695 'type' statement) uniformly with a '(type) Name = T' marker.
claims.rationale: All three alias forms can be detected via the same hover prefix.
claims.excerpt: "value": "```python\n(type) UserIdV3 = str\n```"

claims.claim: Pyright hover returns markdown including a python-fenced signature line such as '(class) Name', '(function) def ...', '(property) ...', '(method) ...', or '(constant) NAME: Literal[...]'.
claims.rationale: Documents the hover format used to discriminate symbol kinds and signatures.
claims.excerpt: "value": "```python\n(property) count: (self: Self@Counter) -> int\n```"

claims.claim: Pyright publishes diagnostics on didOpen without further triggers (broken.py reported 2 diagnostics after open).
claims.rationale: Workspace warmup behavior informs diagnostic collection strategy.
claims.excerpt: Pyright's behavior on didOpen: does it publish diagnostics for opened files without further trigger? - `broken.py`: 2 diagnostic(s)

claims.claim: Pyright returns LSP error -32601 'Unhandled method textDocument/implementation' for implementation requests; it cannot be used to find subclasses.
claims.rationale: Forces the architecture to use an inventory-walk fallback for usedByTypes rather than relying on LSP implementation.
claims.excerpt: "error": "Error: LSP 'textDocument/implementation' error -32601: Unhandled method textDocument/implementation"

claims.claim: Pyright supports codeAction kinds 'quickfix' and 'source.organizeImports' only.
claims.rationale: Limits the range of code actions available from Pyright.
claims.excerpt: "codeActionKinds": [ "quickfix", "source.organizeImports" ]

claims.claim: Pyright textDocument/references returns cross-file reference locations (e.g., Counter referenced from consumer.py and sample.py).
claims.rationale: Confirms references can be used for cross-file usage discovery.
claims.excerpt: "uri": "file:///c%3A/CodeWork/contextatlas/test/fixtures/python/consumer.py", "range": { "start": { "line": 6, "character": 19 }

claims.claim: Pyright typeDefinition can resolve type aliases into typeshed-fallback stubs (e.g., builtins.pyi).
claims.rationale: Indicates typeDefinition may point outside the workspace into stub libraries, relevant for ADR-13's type-info decisions.
claims.excerpt: "uri": "file:///c%3A/CodeWork/contextatlas/node_modules/pyright/dist/typeshed-fallback/stdlib/builtins.pyi"

claims.claim: Pyright's documentSymbol collapses @overload alternates into a single entry for the function.
claims.rationale: Affects how overloaded functions must be represented; cannot rely on documentSymbol to enumerate overload signatures.
claims.excerpt:
### documentSymbol entries named 'parse'

count: 1

claims.claim: Fix-or-defer rubric: fix in v0.2 only when the gap produces wrong/absent symbols on real code AND fix is ≤ 50 LOC with clear regression coverage; otherwise defer or take no action.
claims.rationale: Governs scope decisions for adapter gap fixes.
claims.excerpt: **Fix in v0.2** if: the gap produces wrong or absent symbols on real code shapes in hono or common TS projects, AND fix size is ≤ 50 LOC with clear regression test coverage.

claims.claim: TypeScript integration-test coverage density should mirror the Python adapter's, informed by real TS pathology (hono spot-check).
claims.rationale: Step 4 deliverable to bring TS adapter to parity with Python adapter test density.
claims.excerpt: mirror Python's integration-test coverage density on the TS side, informed by real TS pathology surfaced during a hono spot-check

claims.claim: Adapter methods must not throw on invalid IDs; they must return null / [] / empty shape instead.
claims.rationale: Conformance contract requires graceful handling of invalid IDs.
claims.excerpt: Invalid-ID handling across `getSymbolDetails` / `findReferences` / `getTypeInfo` (returns null / [] / empty shape, doesn't throw)

claims.claim: All language adapters must satisfy the shared conformance contract (symbol-ID shape, missing-file handling, invalid-ID handling, diagnostics, cross-file references, getTypeInfo shape).
claims.rationale: Defines the shared behavioral contract for language adapters; both Python and TypeScript adapters must pass it.
claims.excerpt: Already covered by `src/adapters/conformance.ts` (both adapters pass per Step 9)

claims.claim: getTypeInfo output arrays must be present even when empty.
claims.rationale: Shape contract guarantee for downstream consumers.
claims.excerpt: `getTypeInfo` shape contract (arrays present even when empty)

claims.claim: listSymbols iterates sym.children for container kinds (LSP 2/5/11) and filters children whose mapped kind is 'other' (matches Python policy).
claims.rationale: Fix for Gap 1/2 to surface class, interface, and namespace children consistently with Python adapter policy.
claims.excerpt: `listSymbols` iterates `sym.children` for container kinds (LSP 2/5/11); children whose mapped kind is `"other"` are filtered (matches Python policy)

claims.claim: listSymbols must return [] for missing files rather than throwing.
claims.rationale: Conformance contract for missing-file handling.
claims.excerpt: Missing-file handling (returns [], doesn't throw)

claims.claim: extractDeclarationHeader must track <...> generic depth via character scan; '{' and ';' are terminators only at genericDepth 0.
claims.rationale: Prevents truncation of class signatures with generic defaults like `class Hono<S extends Schema = {}, ...>`.
claims.excerpt: `extractDeclarationHeader` tracks `<...>` depth via character scan; `{` and `;` treated as terminators only at genericDepth 0

claims.claim: extractTypeAliasHeader must terminate either on ';' or before a subsequent line that starts a new top-level declaration (column-0 keyword match).
claims.rationale: Prevents type-alias signatures from bleeding into the next declaration under ASI convention.
claims.excerpt: `extractTypeAliasHeader` adds second termination condition — stops before a subsequent line that starts a new top-level declaration (column-0 keyword match)

claims.claim: Changing the policy for LSP kind=7/8 (mapping to a new SymbolKind or surfacing fields as methods) requires an ADR-01 or ADR-03 amendment.
claims.rationale: Architectural policy is encoded in ADRs; deviating requires formal amendment.
claims.excerpt: the fix surface is extending `mapSymbolKind` to map LSP kind=7 to a new SymbolKind (requires ADR-01 amendment) or relaxing the child-filter policy to surface fields alongside methods (requires ADR-03 policy amendment)

claims.claim: TS fields typed as function (LSP kind=7 Property, kind=8 Field) are intentionally treated as fields and dropped from method listings, mirroring Python's 'drop instance vars' discipline.
claims.rationale: Consistent-policy choice; no Python analog for 'field typed as function'. Changing this would require ADR amendments.
claims.excerpt: the consistent-policy choice is to treat them as fields (dropped) rather than methods (kept)

claims.claim: LanguageAdapter plugin interface contract is defined by ADR-03.
claims.rationale: Identifies authoritative source for adapter interface.
claims.excerpt:
ADR-03-language-adapter-plugin.md`](adr/ADR-03-language-adapter-plugin.md)
  — LanguageAdapter plugin interface contract

claims.claim: Arrow-function exports (`export const fn = () => ...`) currently resolve to kind=variable with empty signature; deferred to v0.3 (not a correctness bug).
claims.rationale: Documents deferred limitation and rationale for not addressing in v0.2.
claims.excerpt: Gap 4 — arrow-function export signatures. `export const fn = (...) =>` resolves to `kind=variable` with empty signature.
